info.title: PR description audit vs branch history with gh cli

parts.text: check the description for the PR for the current branch (use gh cli). audit the description against the git history for this branch
**Preparing commands to inspect current PR**

input.command: git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD && gh pr view --json number,title,body,baseRefName,headRefName,author,commits,files,url
input.description: Get current branch and PR details

chore/docs-drift-guardrails
{"author":{"id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3OTk4MjM=","is_bot":false,"login":"ryderstorm","name":"Damien Storm"},"baseRefName":"main","body":"## Why?

Repository documentation drifted after multiple skill additions and workflow updates (from `v0.3.0` onward), causing stale skill inventory entries and at least one non-existent script reference in docs. This PR adds automated guardrails and aligns core docs with current repository behavior.

## What Changed?

- Added a deterministic documentation drift checker:
  - `scripts/check_docs_drift.py`
  - Verifies `README.md` skill inventory sections match canonical `skills/*/SKILL.md`.
  - Verifies script paths referenced in key docs exist.
- Wired drift checking into pre-commit:
  - Updated `.pre-commit-config.yaml` with local hook `check-docs-drift`.
- Updated repository docs to match current reality:
  - `README.md`
    - Added missing `branch-surgery-pr-split` entries.
    - Added non-interactive install example:
      - `npx -y skills add liatrio-labs/ai-prompts --all`
    - Simplified marker-table note to reduce future drift.
  - `docs/development.md`
    - Clarified `uv` requirement for documented skill-authoring workflow.
    - Added explicit `python scripts/check_docs_drift.py` check.
    - Removed stale troubleshooting guidance referencing a non-existent script.
  - `CONTRIBUTING.md`
    - Replaced template-heavy generic sections with repository-specific quality gates and contribution guidance.
  - `AGENTS.md`
    - Added minimal “Documentation Drift Guardrail” guidance pointing to canonical skill source and checker command.

## Testing

- `python scripts/check_docs_drift.py`
- `pre-commit run --all-files`
- `pre-commit run --files README.md`

All checks passed locally.

## Notes

- This PR intentionally adds enforcement at pre-commit level so drift is caught both locally and in CI (CI runs `pre-commit run --all-files`).
- No runtime product behavior changes; scope is repository tooling and documentation quality.

## Risks / Follow-ups

- Risk: stricter docs drift checks may fail in future PRs that add/remove skills without doc updates.
  - Mitigation: documented the required command in `docs/development.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, and `AGENTS.md`.
- Follow-up: if needed, expand checker coverage to additional docs files beyond current scope.

diff --git a/scripts/check_docs_drift.py b/scripts/check_docs_drift.py
new file mode 100755
index 0000000..a1e5384
--- /dev/null
+++ b/scripts/check_docs_drift.py
@@ -0,0 +1,142 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env python3
+"""Check repository documentation for skill-inventory and script-reference drift."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import re
+from pathlib import Path
+
+REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
+README_PATH = REPO_ROOT / "README.md"
+SKILLS_DIR = REPO_ROOT / "skills"
+DOC_FILES = [
+    REPO_ROOT / "README.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "CONTRIBUTING.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "docs" / "development.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "AGENTS.md",
+]
+
+
+def _extract_between(lines: list[str], start_pattern: str, end_pattern: str | None = None) -> list[str]:
+    start_idx = None
+    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
+        if re.search(start_pattern, line):
+            start_idx = i
+            break
+
+    if start_idx is None:
+        return []
+
+    end_idx = len(lines)
+    if end_pattern is not None:
+        for i in range(start_idx + 1, len(lines)):
+            if re.search(end_pattern, lines[i]):
+                end_idx = i
+                break
+
+    return lines[start_idx + 1 : end_idx]
+
+
+def canonical_skills() -> set[str]:
+    return {p.parent.name for p in SKILLS_DIR.glob("*/SKILL.md")}
+
+
+def readme_skill_bullets(readme_lines: list[str]) -> set[str]:
+    section = _extract_between(
+        readme_lines,
+        r"^Skills are organized in the `skills/` directory by capability:$",
+        r"^### ",
+    )
+    results: set[str] = set()
+    bullet_re = re.compile(r"^- `([a-z0-9-]+)/` -")
+    for line in section:
+        m = bullet_re.match(line.strip())
+        if m:
+            results.add(m.group(1))
+    return results
+
+
+def readme_skill_catalog(readme_lines: list[str]) -> set[str]:
+    section = _extract_between(readme_lines, r"^## Skills Catalog$", r"^## ")
+    results: set[str] = set()
+    row_re = re.compile(r"^\| `([a-z0-9-]+)` \|")
+    for line in section:
+        m = row_re.match(line.strip())
+        if m:
+            results.add(m.group(1))
+    return results
+
+
+def find_script_references() -> tuple[set[str], set[str]]:
+    ref_re = re.compile(r"(?:\./)?(scripts/[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+)")
+    referenced: set[str] = set()
+    missing: set[str] = set()
+
+    for doc in DOC_FILES:
+        text = doc.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
+        for match in ref_re.findall(text):
+            referenced.add(match)
+            if not (REPO_ROOT / match).exists():
+                missing.add(match)
+
+    return referenced, missing
+
+
+def _format_set(label: str, items: set[str]) -> list[str]:
+    if not items:
+        return [f"  {label}: none"]
+    formatted = [f"  {label}:"]
+    formatted.extend(f"    - {item}" for item in sorted(items))
+    return formatted
+
+
+def main() -> int:
+    readme_lines = README_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines()
+
+    canonical = canonical_skills()
+    bullets = readme_skill_bullets(readme_lines)
+    catalog = readme_skill_catalog(readme_lines)
+    referenced_scripts, missing_scripts = find_script_references()
+
+    failures: list[str] = []
+
+    bullet_missing = canonical - bullets
+    bullet_extra = bullets - canonical
+    if bullet_missing or bullet_extra:
+        failures.append("README skills bullet list drift detected:")
+        failures.extend(_format_set("missing entries", bullet_missing))
+        failures.extend(_format_set("stale/extra entries", bullet_extra))
+
+    catalog_missing = canonical - catalog
+    catalog_extra = catalog - canonical
+    if catalog_missing or catalog_extra:
+        failures.append("README skills catalog drift detected:")
+        failures.extend(_format_set("missing entries", catalog_missing))
+        failures.extend(_format_set("stale/extra entries", catalog_extra))
+
+    if missing_scripts:
+        failures.append("Missing script references detected in docs:")
+        failures.extend(f"    - {item}" for item in sorted(missing_scripts))
+
+    if failures:
+        print("Documentation drift check failed.")
+        print()
+        for line in failures:
+            print(line)
+        print()
+        print("Canonical skills source: skills/*/SKILL.md")
+        print("Scanned script-reference docs:")
+        for doc in DOC_FILES:
+            print(f"  - {doc.relative_to(REPO_ROOT)}")
+        print("Referenced script paths found:")
+        for item in sorted(referenced_scripts):
+            print(f"  - {item}")
+        return 1
+
+    print("Documentation drift check passed.")
+    print(f"Canonical skills: {', '.join(sorted(canonical))}")
+    return 0
+
+
+if __name__ == "__main__":
+    raise SystemExit(main())

+++ b/scripts/check_docs_drift.py
@@ -0,0 +1,142 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env python3
+"""Check repository documentation for skill-inventory and script-reference drift."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import re
+from pathlib import Path
+
+REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
+README_PATH = REPO_ROOT / "README.md"
+SKILLS_DIR = REPO_ROOT / "skills"
+DOC_FILES = [
+    REPO_ROOT / "README.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "CONTRIBUTING.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "docs" / "development.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "AGENTS.md",
+]
+
+
+def _extract_between(lines: list[str], start_pattern: str, end_pattern: str | None = None) -> list[str]:
+    start_idx = None
+    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
+        if re.search(start_pattern, line):
+            start_idx = i
+            break
+
+    if start_idx is None:
+        return []
+
+    end_idx = len(lines)
+    if end_pattern is not None:
+        for i in range(start_idx + 1, len(lines)):
+            if re.search(end_pattern, lines[i]):
+                end_idx = i
+                break
+
+    return lines[start_idx + 1 : end_idx]
+
+
+def canonical_skills() -> set[str]:
+    return {p.parent.name for p in SKILLS_DIR.glob("*/SKILL.md")}
+
+
+def readme_skill_bullets(readme_lines: list[str]) -> set[str]:
+    section = _extract_between(
+        readme_lines,
+        r"^Skills are organized in the `skills/` directory by capability:$",
+        r"^### ",
+    )
+    results: set[str] = set()
+    bullet_re = re.compile(r"^- `([a-z0-9-]+)/` -")
+    for line in section:
+        m = bullet_re.match(line.strip())
+        if m:
+            results.add(m.group(1))
+    return results
+
+
+def readme_skill_catalog(readme_lines: list[str]) -> set[str]:
+    section = _extract_between(readme_lines, r"^## Skills Catalog$", r"^## ")
+    results: set[str] = set()
+    row_re = re.compile(r"^\| `([a-z0-9-]+)` \|")
+    for line in section:
+        m = row_re.match(line.strip())
+        if m:
+            results.add(m.group(1))
+    return results
+
+
+def find_script_references() -> tuple[set[str], set[str]]:
+    ref_re = re.compile(r"(?:\./)?(scripts/[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+)")
+    referenced: set[str] = set()
+    missing: set[str] = set()
+
+    for doc in DOC_FILES:
+        text = doc.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
+        for match in ref_re.findall(text):
+            referenced.add(match)
+            if not (REPO_ROOT / match).exists():
+                missing.add(match)
+
+    return referenced, missing
+
+
+def _format_set(label: str, items: set[str]) -> list[str]:
+    if not items:
+        return [f"  {label}: none"]
+    formatted = [f"  {label}:"]
+    formatted.extend(f"    - {item}" for item in sorted(items))
+    return formatted
+
+
+def main() -> int:
+    readme_lines = README_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines()
+
+    canonical = canonical_skills()
+    bullets = readme_skill_bullets(readme_lines)
+    catalog = readme_skill_catalog(readme_lines)
+    referenced_scripts, missing_scripts = find_script_references()
+
+    failures: list[str] = []
+
+    bullet_missing = canonical - bullets
+    bullet_extra = bullets - canonical
+    if bullet_missing or bullet_extra:
+        failures.append("README skills bullet list drift detected:")
+        failures.extend(_format_set("missing entries", bullet_missing))
+        failures.extend(_format_set("stale/extra entries", bullet_extra))
+
+    catalog_missing = canonical - catalog
+    catalog_extra = catalog - canonical
+    if catalog_missing or catalog_extra:
+        failures.append("README skills catalog drift detected:")
+        failures.extend(_format_set("missing entries", catalog_missing))
+        failures.extend(_format_set("stale/extra entries", catalog_extra))
+
+    if missing_scripts:
+        failures.append("Missing script references detected in docs:")
+        failures.extend(f"    - {item}" for item in sorted(missing_scripts))
+
+    if failures:
+        print("Documentation drift check failed.")
+        print()
+        for line in failures:
+            print(line)
+        print()
+        print("Canonical skills source: skills/*/SKILL.md")
+        print("Scanned script-reference docs:")
+        for doc in DOC_FILES:
+            print(f"  - {doc.relative_to(REPO_ROOT)}")
+        print("Referenced script paths found:")
+        for item in sorted(referenced_scripts):
+            print(f"  - {item}")
+        return 1
+
+    print("Documentation drift check passed.")
+    print(f"Canonical skills: {', '.join(sorted(canonical))}")
+    return 0
+
+
+if __name__ == "__main__":
+    raise SystemExit(main())

parts.text: **Preparing audit summary outline**

🤖🎵
I checked PR `#19` (`https://github.com/liatrio-labs/ai-prompts/pull/19`) via `gh` and audited the description against `main..HEAD` commit history and diff.

**Audit**
- Verdict: the PR description is **accurate and consistent** with branch history (3 commits) and changed files.
- Commit history matches scope: `chore(repo): add docs drift checker and guardrails`, `docs(contributing): align guide with repo quality gates`, `docs(readme): add non-interactive npx skills example`.
- File-level claims align with diff in `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, `scripts/check_docs_drift.py`, `README.md`, `docs/development.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, and `AGENTS.md`.
- Specific claims confirmed: added docs drift checker + pre-commit hook, added `branch-surgery-pr-split` entries in `README.md`, added non-interactive `npx -y ... --all`, added drift guidance in `AGENTS.md`, and removed stale script reference guidance in `docs/development.md`.
- No contradictory changes found in git history (no runtime/product code changes).

**Notes**
- The “All checks passed locally” testing statement is plausible but not provable from git history alone; it’s a runtime claim, not a commit artifact.
- Otherwise, the PR narrative maps cleanly to the actual branch delta.

# Review Branch Against Base

This workflow performs an exhaustive review of the current branch against a base branch (default `main`). It collects comprehensive git data and writes outputs to temp files in the system temp directory so the AI can read and analyze them in chunks.

## Context Marker

Always begin your response with all active emoji markers, in the order they were introduced.

Format:  "<marker1><marker2><marker3>\n<response>"

The marker for this instruction is:  🔍

## Guidelines

Act as a senior code reviewer with 15+ years of experience across multiple domains including security engineering, performance optimization, and software architecture. Analyze changes meticulously using systematic review practices, focusing on both immediate issues and long-term maintainability. Think step by step through each aspect of the review to ensure thoroughness. Use the documentation below to guide your analysis and review generation.

### Review Categories

| Category | Focus Areas |
|----------|-------------|
| Code Correctness | Logic errors, edge cases, error handling, null/undefined checks |
| Security | Injection vulnerabilities, authentication issues, data exposure, input validation |
| Performance | Algorithm efficiency, database queries, memory usage, caching opportunities |
| Code Quality | Readability, maintainability, DRY, single responsibility |
| Architecture | Design patterns, coupling, cohesion, scalability |
| Testing | Coverage, test quality, edge cases |
| Documentation | Comments, API docs, README updates |
| Dependencies | New/updated deps, security |
| Accessibility | WCAG, keyboard navigation, screen readers |
| Best Practices | Language/framework/team conventions |

### Severity Levels

| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Critical | Must fix before merge (e.g., security, data loss, breaking changes) |
| High | Significant issues to address (performance, error handling, accessibility) |
| Medium | Important improvements (duplication, missing tests, naming) |
| Low | Nice-to-have (style, minor optimizations, docs) |
| Info | Observations and positives |

## Overall Process

Follow the steps below to produce a review report. Think hard about each step and be sure to go through each step to cover the entire process. Refer to the section below for more details on each step.

1. Ensure the working tree is clean (no unstaged or uncommitted changes). If not clean, ask the user to commit or stash.
2. Collect the git information and write it to a temp directory.
3. Thoroughly review the git information and create your analysis.
4. Present the analysis to the user as a review report.

## Safety Checks

1. Confirm Git is initialized in the current directory.
2. Ensure the working tree is clean (no unstaged or uncommitted changes). If not clean, ask the user to commit or stash.
3. Assume the repository state is already set up correctly; default base is `main`.

## Collect Repository Context

Run the following to gather all review artifacts into temp files:

```bash
set -euo pipefail

# Base branch to compare against (default to main)
# You can override `BASE_BRANCH` via environment (e.g., BASE_BRANCH=develop)
: "${BASE_BRANCH:=main}"

# Create a dedicated output directory for this review session
OUTDIR=$(mktemp -d -t review-branch)

# Record the chosen base for later reference
printf "%s\n" "$BASE_BRANCH" >"$OUTDIR/base_branch.txt"

# 1) Basic repo state (from squash-merge workflow)
( git status ) >"$OUTDIR/status.txt"
( git branch --show-current ) >"$OUTDIR/current_branch.txt"
( git rev-parse --show-toplevel ) >"$OUTDIR/repo_root.txt"

# 2) Commit history on branch vs base (from squash-merge workflow)
( git log "$BASE_BRANCH"..HEAD --oneline ) >"$OUTDIR/commits_oneline.txt"
( git log "$BASE_BRANCH"..HEAD --format='format:%B%n----%n' ) >"$OUTDIR/commits_full.txt"
( git log --pretty=format:'%h %s' --no-merges "$BASE_BRANCH"..HEAD ) >"$OUTDIR/commits_pretty.txt"

# 3) Change scope and stats (from squash-merge workflow)
( git diff "$BASE_BRANCH"...HEAD --stat ) >"$OUTDIR/diff_stat.txt"
( git diff --name-only --diff-filter=ACMRT "$BASE_BRANCH"...HEAD ) >"$OUTDIR/changed_files.txt"

# 4) Minimal manifest of outputs for the AI to read
cat >"$OUTDIR/MANIFEST.txt" <<EOF
Artifacts directory: $OUTDIR
Base branch: $(cat "$OUTDIR/base_branch.txt" 2>/dev/null || echo "$BASE_BRANCH")

Core context files:
- repo_root.txt
- current_branch.txt
- status.txt
- base_branch.txt

Commit history:
- commits_oneline.txt
- commits_full.txt
- commits_pretty.txt

Change scope and stats:
- diff_stat.txt
- changed_files.txt
EOF

# 5) Print the OUTDIR path for convenience
printf "\nReview artifacts saved to: %s\n" "$OUTDIR"
```

## AI Review Instructions

Read the artifacts from `MANIFEST.txt` and analyze in chunks. Focus on:

- Scope: files, directories, languages, and components affected.
- Summary: clear overview of what changed and why (infer from commits and diffs).
- Metrics: files changed, insertions/deletions, biggest files, hotspots.
- Risks: large deletions, config or infra changes, secrets, binary or lockfiles, migrations, breaking changes.
- Testing: presence/absence of tests; coverage gaps inferred from changes.
- Security: obvious issues, dependency changes, secrets, unsafe patterns.
- Performance: hotspots, N^2 risks, heavy loops, large payloads.
- Documentation: README/CHANGELOG/docs updates needed.
- Conventions: Conventional Commits compliance, lint/test updates needed.

### Suggested Reading Order

1. `status.txt`, `current_branch.txt`, `base_branch.txt`.
2. `diff_stat.txt`, `changed_files.txt`.
3. `commits_oneline.txt`, then `commits_full.txt` (chunked by `----`).

### Review Process (Phases)

#### Phase 1: Context Gathering

- Understand purpose and scope of changes
- Identify affected components and dependencies
- Review recent commit history for context
- Check related issues/PRs if referenced in commits

#### Phase 2: Systematic Analysis

- Analyze each changed file methodically (use `changed_files.txt` as driver)
- Apply all review categories to significant changes
- Consider interactions between components
- Evaluate impact on existing functionality

#### Phase 3: Security Deep Dive

- Check for common vulnerability patterns
- Validate user inputs and data flows
- Review authn/authz logic
- Assess handling of sensitive data

#### Phase 4: Quality Assessment

- Code organization and structure
- SOLID, naming, clarity
- Error handling completeness

#### Phase 5: Verification

- Double-check critical findings
- Validate suggested improvements
- Consider alternatives and trade-offs
- Ensure recommendations are actionable

### Additional Considerations

For frontend: browser compatibility, responsive design, Core Web Vitals, accessibility.

For backend: API contract changes, migration safety, backward compatibility, rate limiting and security headers.

For infra: rollback, monitoring/alerting, resource utilization, security groups.

## Output Format

- Produce a concise but complete review report
- Keep the main report to ~400-800 words.
- Provide a short bullet list of top 5 items at the top.
- Reference files and directories with backticks.

Structure your review like this:

```markdown
# Code Review Report

## Executive Summary
[2-3 sentences summarizing overall code health and key concerns]

## Review Metrics
- Files Changed: X
- Lines Added: Y
- Lines Removed: Z
- Total Issues Found: N (Critical: X, High: Y, Medium: Z, Low: W)

## Critical Issues 🚨
[Issues that must be fixed before merge]

## High Priority Issues ⚠️
[Significant issues that should be addressed]

## Medium Priority Issues 📝
[Important improvements needed]

## Low Priority Suggestions 💡
[Nice-to-have improvements]

## Positive Observations 👍
[Good practices and improvements noted]

## Recommendations
[Specific next steps and priorities]
```

## Interactive Mode

If clarification is needed:

1. Ask specific questions about intent or requirements
2. Request additional business context
3. Suggest targeted tests or checks to run
4. Offer deep dives on complex areas

## Quality Checklist

Before finalizing the review, ensure:

- [ ] All changed files were analyzed
- [ ] Security implications considered
- [ ] Performance impact evaluated
- [ ] Recommendations are specific and actionable
- [ ] Positive feedback included where appropriate

## Example Output

```text
Review artifacts saved to: /var/folders/.../T/review-branch.xxxxxx

Top findings:
- Touches 27 files across `api/`, `web/`, `infra/`. 2 migrations detected.
- Large addition in `api/users.go` (+420) and `web/UserTable.tsx` (+350).
- Risk: config drift in `infra/terraform/*.tf`, consider a plan run.
- Tests missing for `api/users.go` paths; only UI tests added.
- Breaking change in `POST /v2/users` contract; docs need update.
```

index 0000000..a1e5384
--- /dev/null
+++ b/scripts/check_docs_drift.py
@@ -0,0 +1,142 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env python3
+"""Check repository documentation for skill-inventory and script-reference drift."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import re
+from pathlib import Path
+
+REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
+README_PATH = REPO_ROOT / "README.md"
+SKILLS_DIR = REPO_ROOT / "skills"
+DOC_FILES = [
+    REPO_ROOT / "README.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "CONTRIBUTING.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "docs" / "development.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "AGENTS.md",
+]
+
+
+def _extract_between(lines: list[str], start_pattern: str, end_pattern: str | None = None) -> list[str]:
+    start_idx = None
+    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
+        if re.search(start_pattern, line):
+            start_idx = i
+            break
+
+    if start_idx is None:
+        return []
+
+    end_idx = len(lines)
+    if end_pattern is not None:
+        for i in range(start_idx + 1, len(lines)):
+            if re.search(end_pattern, lines[i]):
+                end_idx = i
+                break
+
+    return lines[start_idx + 1 : end_idx]
+
+
+def canonical_skills() -> set[str]:
+    return {p.parent.name for p in SKILLS_DIR.glob("*/SKILL.md")}
+
+
+def readme_skill_bullets(readme_lines: list[str]) -> set[str]:
+    section = _extract_between(
+        readme_lines,
+        r"^Skills are organized in the `skills/` directory by capability:$",
+        r"^### ",
+    )
+    results: set[str] = set()
+    bullet_re = re.compile(r"^- `([a-z0-9-]+)/` -")
+    for line in section:
+        m = bullet_re.match(line.strip())
+        if m:
+            results.add(m.group(1))
+    return results
+
+
+def readme_skill_catalog(readme_lines: list[str]) -> set[str]:
+    section = _extract_between(readme_lines, r"^## Skills Catalog$", r"^## ")
+    results: set[str] = set()
+    row_re = re.compile(r"^\| `([a-z0-9-]+)` \|")
+    for line in section:
+        m = row_re.match(line.strip())
+        if m:
+            results.add(m.group(1))
+    return results
+
+
+def find_script_references() -> tuple[set[str], set[str]]:
+    ref_re = re.compile(r"(?:\./)?(scripts/[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+)")
+    referenced: set[str] = set()
+    missing: set[str] = set()
+
+    for doc in DOC_FILES:
+        text = doc.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
+        for match in ref_re.findall(text):
+            referenced.add(match)
+            if not (REPO_ROOT / match).exists():
+                missing.add(match)
+
+    return referenced, missing
+
+
+def _format_set(label: str, items: set[str]) -> list[str]:
+    if not items:
+        return [f"  {label}: none"]
+    formatted = [f"  {label}:"]
+    formatted.extend(f"    - {item}" for item in sorted(items))
+    return formatted
+
+
+def main() -> int:
+    readme_lines = README_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines()
+
+    canonical = canonical_skills()
+    bullets = readme_skill_bullets(readme_lines)
+    catalog = readme_skill_catalog(readme_lines)
+    referenced_scripts, missing_scripts = find_script_references()
+
+    failures: list[str] = []
+
+    bullet_missing = canonical - bullets
+    bullet_extra = bullets - canonical
+    if bullet_missing or bullet_extra:
+        failures.append("README skills bullet list drift detected:")
+        failures.extend(_format_set("missing entries", bullet_missing))
+        failures.extend(_format_set("stale/extra entries", bullet_extra))
+
+    catalog_missing = canonical - catalog
+    catalog_extra = catalog - canonical
+    if catalog_missing or catalog_extra:
+        failures.append("README skills catalog drift detected:")
+        failures.extend(_format_set("missing entries", catalog_missing))
+        failures.extend(_format_set("stale/extra entries", catalog_extra))
+
+    if missing_scripts:
+        failures.append("Missing script references detected in docs:")
+        failures.extend(f"    - {item}" for item in sorted(missing_scripts))
+
+    if failures:
+        print("Documentation drift check failed.")
+        print()
+        for line in failures:
+            print(line)
+        print()
+        print("Canonical skills source: skills/*/SKILL.md")
+        print("Scanned script-reference docs:")
+        for doc in DOC_FILES:
+            print(f"  - {doc.relative_to(REPO_ROOT)}")
+        print("Referenced script paths found:")
+        for item in sorted(referenced_scripts):
+            print(f"  - {item}")
+        return 1
+
+    print("Documentation drift check passed.")
+    print(f"Canonical skills: {', '.join(sorted(canonical))}")
+    return 0
+
+
+if __name__ == "__main__":
+    raise SystemExit(main())

new file mode 100755
index 0000000..a1e5384
--- /dev/null
+++ b/scripts/check_docs_drift.py
@@ -0,0 +1,142 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env python3
+"""Check repository documentation for skill-inventory and script-reference drift."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import re
+from pathlib import Path
+
+REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
+README_PATH = REPO_ROOT / "README.md"
+SKILLS_DIR = REPO_ROOT / "skills"
+DOC_FILES = [
+    REPO_ROOT / "README.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "CONTRIBUTING.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "docs" / "development.md",
+    REPO_ROOT / "AGENTS.md",
+]
+
+
+def _extract_between(lines: list[str], start_pattern: str, end_pattern: str | None = None) -> list[str]:
+    start_idx = None
+    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
+        if re.search(start_pattern, line):
+            start_idx = i
+            break
+
+    if start_idx is None:
+        return []
+
+    end_idx = len(lines)
+    if end_pattern is not None:
+        for i in range(start_idx + 1, len(lines)):
+            if re.search(end_pattern, lines[i]):
+                end_idx = i
+                break
+
+    return lines[start_idx + 1 : end_idx]
+
+
+def canonical_skills() -> set[str]:
+    return {p.parent.name for p in SKILLS_DIR.glob("*/SKILL.md")}
+
+
+def readme_skill_bullets(readme_lines: list[str]) -> set[str]:
+    section = _extract_between(
+        readme_lines,
+        r"^Skills are organized in the `skills/` directory by capability:$",
+        r"^### ",
+    )
+    results: set[str] = set()
+    bullet_re = re.compile(r"^- `([a-z0-9-]+)/` -")
+    for line in section:
+        m = bullet_re.match(line.strip())
+        if m:
+            results.add(m.group(1))
+    return results
+
+
+def readme_skill_catalog(readme_lines: list[str]) -> set[str]:
+    section = _extract_between(readme_lines, r"^## Skills Catalog$", r"^## ")
+    results: set[str] = set()
+    row_re = re.compile(r"^\| `([a-z0-9-]+)` \|")
+    for line in section:
+        m = row_re.match(line.strip())
+        if m:
+            results.add(m.group(1))
+    return results
+
+
+def find_script_references() -> tuple[set[str], set[str]]:
+    ref_re = re.compile(r"(?:\./)?(scripts/[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+)")
+    referenced: set[str] = set()
+    missing: set[str] = set()
+
+    for doc in DOC_FILES:
+        text = doc.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
+        for match in ref_re.findall(text):
+            referenced.add(match)
+            if not (REPO_ROOT / match).exists():
+                missing.add(match)
+
+    return referenced, missing
+
+
+def _format_set(label: str, items: set[str]) -> list[str]:
+    if not items:
+        return [f"  {label}: none"]
+    formatted = [f"  {label}:"]
+    formatted.extend(f"    - {item}" for item in sorted(items))
+    return formatted
+
+
+def main() -> int:
+    readme_lines = README_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines()
+
+    canonical = canonical_skills()
+    bullets = readme_skill_bullets(readme_lines)
+    catalog = readme_skill_catalog(readme_lines)
+    referenced_scripts, missing_scripts = find_script_references()
+
+    failures: list[str] = []
+
+    bullet_missing = canonical - bullets
+    bullet_extra = bullets - canonical
+    if bullet_missing or bullet_extra:
+        failures.append("README skills bullet list drift detected:")
+        failures.extend(_format_set("missing entries", bullet_missing))
+        failures.extend(_format_set("stale/extra entries", bullet_extra))
+
+    catalog_missing = canonical - catalog
+    catalog_extra = catalog - canonical
+    if catalog_missing or catalog_extra:
+        failures.append("README skills catalog drift detected:")
+        failures.extend(_format_set("missing entries", catalog_missing))
+        failures.extend(_format_set("stale/extra entries", catalog_extra))
+
+    if missing_scripts:
+        failures.append("Missing script references detected in docs:")
+        failures.extend(f"    - {item}" for item in sorted(missing_scripts))
+
+    if failures:
+        print("Documentation drift check failed.")
+        print()
+        for line in failures:
+            print(line)
+        print()
+        print("Canonical skills source: skills/*/SKILL.md")
+        print("Scanned script-reference docs:")
+        for doc in DOC_FILES:
+            print(f"  - {doc.relative_to(REPO_ROOT)}")
+        print("Referenced script paths found:")
+        for item in sorted(referenced_scripts):
+            print(f"  - {item}")
+        return 1
+
+    print("Documentation drift check passed.")
+    print(f"Canonical skills: {', '.join(sorted(canonical))}")
+    return 0
+
+
+if __name__ == "__main__":
+    raise SystemExit(main())

parts.text: **Preparing structured code review report**

🤖🎵🔍
Top findings:
- Scope is focused and coherent: 6 files changed, mostly documentation plus one new guardrail script in `scripts/check_docs_drift.py`.
- No Critical/High defects found; branch is low risk and consistent with commit intent.
- New pre-commit integration in `.pre-commit-config.yaml` correctly enforces docs drift checks for relevant files.
- `scripts/check_docs_drift.py` is deterministic and readable, but has a maintainability gap around hard-coded README section anchors.
- Validation evidence is strong: working tree clean, `python scripts/check_docs_drift.py` passes, and pre-commit hooks pass on changed files.

# Code Review Report

## Executive Summary
This branch is a solid documentation-governance improvement that adds automated drift detection and aligns contributor-facing docs with repository reality. The implementation is small, focused, and easy to reason about, with no obvious correctness, security, or performance risks. The only notable concerns are medium/low maintainability opportunities in how the checker parses markdown sections and how scope evolves over time.

## Review Metrics
- Files Changed: 6
- Lines Added: 192
- Lines Removed: 111
- Total Issues Found: 2 (Critical: 0, High: 0, Medium: 1, Low: 1)

## Critical Issues 🚨
- None found.

## High Priority Issues ⚠️
- None found.

## Medium Priority Issues 📝
- `scripts/check_docs_drift.py`: README section parsing is anchor-string fragile.  
  The checker depends on exact headings/phrasing (`Skills are organized...`, `## Skills Catalog`) via regex in `_extract_between`, `readme_skill_bullets`, and `readme_skill_catalog`. Minor editorial wording changes in `README.md` can break enforcement even if content is correct.  
  Recommendation: add clearer sentinel markers in `README.md` (for example HTML comments) and parse by marker boundaries, or make heading matching more resilient with fallback diagnostics.

## Low Priority Suggestions 💡
- `scripts/check_docs_drift.py`: scope configurability could reduce future churn.  
  `DOC_FILES` is hard-coded and script-reference regex is intentionally broad. As docs grow, this may require repeated script edits for additional docs that should be checked.  
  Recommendation: support a small config source (YAML/TOML) or a documented convention for “checked docs” to simplify extension and reduce PR noise.

## Positive Observations 👍
- Good governance improvement: pre-commit hook in `.pre-commit-config.yaml` enforces drift checks at commit time.
- Documentation updates are internally consistent across `README.md`, `docs/development.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, and `AGENTS.md`.
- Commit history is clean and conventional (`docs(...)`, `chore(...)`) and aligns with actual diff scope.
- Security posture is unaffected (no secrets, auth, dependency, or runtime attack-surface changes).
- Performance impact is negligible; script operates on a small number of markdown files and simple regex/set operations.

## Recommendations
1. Merge after optional hardening of markdown-section parsing in `scripts/check_docs_drift.py`.
2. Add one lightweight test fixture or CI smoke check for the checker behavior against a known sample to guard against regressions.
3. Consider documenting explicit “machine-checked sections” in `README.md` so future editorial edits don’t accidentally trip the guardrail.
4. Keep this guardrail in sync when adding new docs that reference scripts/skills (current behavior is good, just needs a scaling path).

Review artifacts path used: `/tmp/review-branch.qb6CwK`.

67: - `.github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml`
68: - `.github/ruleset-config.json`
69: - `.github/renovate.json`
70: 
71: ### 4) Repo hygiene
72: 
73: Added/updated:
74: 
75: - `.gitignore` (expanded from minimal ignores to practical baseline)
76: - `.env.example` (documented integration placeholders)
77: - `.markdownlint.yaml` (disabled `MD060` to avoid table-style churn in existing docs)
78: 
79: ### 5) Validation and iterative fixes completed
80: 
81: Executed and resolved:
82: 
83: - `markdownlint --fix` and verification runs
84: - `pre-commit run --all-files` until clean
85: - CI run monitoring and remediation when branch CI did not trigger
86: - workflow trigger update so feature branches run CI
87: 
88: ## Delta From `base-repo-template` (Current State)
89: 
90: ### Intentionally adapted for docs-first maturity
91: 
92: - `ci.yml` uses a docs-first placeholder test job (`Run Tests`) and pre-commit-driven lint job (`Run Linting`) instead of language build/test commands.
93: - `.releaserc.toml` currently keeps:
94:   - `version_variables = []`
95:   - `assets = []`
96: 
97: This is acceptable for current docs-first phase but should be finalized once implementation begins.
98: 
99: ### Strict parity improvements applied
100: 
101: - release workflow aligned to Octo STS pattern
102: - Chainguard identity file added and customized to this repo
103: - CODEOWNERS moved from placeholder to concrete ownership patterns
104: - remote settings/rules aligned to known-good baseline using `gh api`
105: 
106: ### Additional settings parity with known-good repo
107: 
108: Remote settings and rules were aligned to `open-source-project-template`, including:
109: 
110: - `allow_auto_merge=true`
111: - `allow_update_branch=true`
112: - squash message/title defaults
113: - `has_wiki=false`
114: - ruleset integration bypass actor (`actor_id: 801323`) added
115: 
116: ## `gh` CLI Process and Commands Used
117: 
118: The following command patterns were used to inspect, compare, and remediate repository settings/rules.
119: 
120: ### Inspect repository settings
121: 
122: ```bash
123: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/open-source-project-template --jq '{...}'
124: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker --jq '{...}'
125: ```
126: 
127: ### Inspect rulesets
128: 
129: ```bash
130: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/open-source-project-template/rulesets
131: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker/rulesets
132: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/open-source-project-template/rulesets/9731318
133: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker/rulesets/13354192
134: ```
135: 
136: ### Inspect Actions permissions
137: 
138: ```bash
139: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/open-source-project-template/actions/permissions
140: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker/actions/permissions
141: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/open-source-project-template/actions/permissions/workflow
142: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker/actions/permissions/workflow
143: ```
144: 
145: ### Apply repository settings (PATCH)
146: 
147: ```bash
148: gh api -X PATCH repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker \
149:   -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
150:   -H "X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28" \
151:   -F has_issues=true \
152:   -F has_wiki=true \
153:   -F allow_squash_merge=true \
154:   -F allow_merge_commit=false \
155:   -F allow_rebase_merge=false \
156:   -F delete_branch_on_merge=true
157: ```
158: 
159: Later parity alignment pass:
160: 
161: ```bash
162: gh api -X PATCH repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker \
163:   -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
164:   -H "X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28" \
165:   -F allow_auto_merge=true \
166:   -F allow_update_branch=true \
167:   -F use_squash_pr_title_as_default=true \
168:   -F squash_merge_commit_title=PR_TITLE \
169:   -F squash_merge_commit_message=BLANK \
170:   -F has_wiki=false
171: ```
172: 
173: ### Apply ruleset from file (POST)
174: 
175: ```bash
176: gh api -X POST repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker/rulesets \
177:   -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
178:   -H "X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28" \
179:   --input .github/ruleset-config.json
180: ```
181: 
182: ### Update existing ruleset with integration bypass actor (PUT)
183: 
184: ```bash
185: gh api repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker/rulesets/13354192 \
186:   --jq '{name,target,enforcement,conditions,rules,bypass_actors}' > /tmp/forge-ruleset-base.json
187: 
188: jq '.bypass_actors = ((.bypass_actors + [{"actor_type":"Integration","actor_id":801323,"bypass_mode":"always"}]) | unique_by(.actor_type, .actor_id))' \
189:   /tmp/forge-ruleset-base.json > /tmp/forge-ruleset-updated.json
190: 
191: gh api -X PUT repos/liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker/rulesets/13354192 \
192:   -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
193:   -H "X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28" \
194:   --input /tmp/forge-ruleset-updated.json
195: ```
196: 
197: ## Verification Performed
198: 
199: - Local file-level:
200:   - `markdownlint --fix` and `markdownlint` checks on updated markdown
201:   - `pre-commit run --all-files` passing
202:   - placeholder token scans for `{OWNER}`, `{REPO}`, `{ORGANIZATION}`, `{TEAM_NAME}`
203: - Remote GitHub:
204:   - repo settings queried and confirmed
205:   - ruleset existence/enforcement/rules queried and confirmed
206:   - bypass actors queried and confirmed against known-good
207:   - CI workflow run monitored to successful completion
208: 
209: ## Important Caveats / Follow-ups
210: 
211: - Workflow visibility on default branch:
212:   - if `Semantic Release` or additional workflows are not visible in `gh workflow list`, ensure their workflow files are merged to `main`.
213: - Semantic release stamping:
214:   - once implementation starts, configure `.releaserc.toml` `version_variables` (or equivalent) and lockfile assets/build behavior.
215: - CODEOWNERS:
216:   - current broad org ownership is functional; may later be refined to team handles.
217: 
218: ## Why This State Is a Good Template Target
219: 
220: This state provides a practical, enforceable baseline that works for a docs-first repo and scales cleanly into implementation:
221: 
222: - governance and collaboration are standardized early
223: - quality/security checks are mandatory from day zero
224: - CI contract and ruleset checks are enforced consistently
225: - release automation path is pre-wired with secure org auth model
226: - remote settings parity is explicitly verified with `gh api`
227: 
228: ## Converting the Repo Template into a Reusable Agent Skill
229: 
230: This section describes how to convert `base-repo-template` from a static repository template into an executable agent skill that can:
231: 
232: - initialize a new repository to baseline standards, or
233: - upgrade an existing repository to the same target baseline.
234: 
235: ### Desired Skill Outcome
236: 
237: Given a target repository and mode (`new_repo` or `upgrade_repo`), the skill should:
238: 
239: 1. audit current state,
240: 2. compute a delta against template baseline,
241: 3. apply file and GitHub settings/rules changes safely,
242: 4. validate with checks,
243: 5. produce a machine- and human-readable report.
244: 
245: ### Why this should be a skill (not just a template)
246: 
247: - Template repos only help at creation time.
248: - Many repos need in-place remediation over time.
249: - A skill can enforce repeatable workflows, ask required questions, and apply settings through APIs.
250: 
251: ## Cross-Vendor Best Practices to Use in the Skill Design
252: 
253: ### OpenAI guidance to apply
254: 
255: References:
256: 
257: - <https://developers.openai.com/tracks/building-agents/>
258: - <https://openai.com/business/guides-and-resources/a-practical-guide-to-building-ai-agents/>
259: 
260: Key takeaways to encode:
261: 
262: - Agent = **instructions + guardrails + tools**.
263: - Keep orchestration explicit: multi-step tool loops, handoffs, and guardrails.
264: - Use tools deliberately for actions and retrieval; do not rely on model memory for source of truth.
265: - Add focused evaluations for scoped goals to prevent regressions as the skill evolves.
266: 
267: ### Anthropic guidance to apply
268: 
269: Reference:
270: 
271: - <https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/claude-prompting-best-practices>
272: 
273: Key takeaways to encode:
274: 
275: - Be explicit and direct in system/developer instructions.
276: - Use clear structure (for example, tagged or sectioned prompt blocks) so the model separates rules, context, examples, and output contracts.
277: - Include examples for ambiguous tasks.
278: - Use parallel tool calls only for independent actions; keep dependent calls sequential.
279: - Avoid over-aggressive prompting that causes tool over-triggering.
280: 
281: ### Vercel guidance to apply
282: 
283: Reference:
284: 
285: - <https://sdk.vercel.ai/docs/ai-sdk-core/tools-and-tool-calling>
286: 
287: Key takeaways to encode:
288: 
289: - Tool definitions should have strong input schemas (`inputSchema`) and clear descriptions.
290: - Use strict tool validation where supported (`strict: true`) for reliability.
291: - Add approval gates (`needsApproval`) for high-impact operations.
292: - Support multi-step tool loops for plan -> apply -> verify pipelines.
293: 
294: ### GitHub guidance to apply
295: 
296: References:
297: 
298: - <https://docs.github.com/en/actions/sharing-automations/reusing-workflows>
299: - <https://cli.github.com/manual/gh_api>
300: - <https://docs.github.com/en/rest/repos/rules>
301: 
302: Key takeaways to encode:
303: 
304: - Treat automation as reusable and parameterized.
305: - Use least-privilege permissions; do not silently elevate.
306: - Pin workflow/action references where possible (prefer stable version pins, SHA for third-party).
307: - Use `gh api` with explicit headers for rules endpoints and JSON payload files for complex bodies.
308: 
309: ### Google guidance to apply
310: 
311: Reference:
312: 
313: - <https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/live-api/best-practices>
314: 
315: Key takeaways to encode:
316: 
317: - System instructions should be ordered and explicit:
318:   1. persona/role,
319:   2. conversational or workflow rules,
320:   3. tool invocation conditions,
321:   4. guardrails.
322: - Tool invocation conditions should be spelled out in concrete, stepwise language.
323: - Prefer concise prompts with prompt-chaining for complex operations.
324: 
325: ## Skill Contract (What the Skill Must Accept and Return)
326: 
327: ### Inputs
328: 
329: - `target_repo` (required): local path or `owner/repo`.
330: - `mode` (required): `new_repo` or `upgrade_repo`.
331: - `apply_remote_settings` (default `true`): whether to call `gh api`.
332: - `strict_parity` (default `true`): whether to enforce known-good parity deltas.
333: - `dry_run` (default `false`): audit and report only.
334: - `base_template_repo` (default local path): source of truth for file-level baseline.
335: - `known_good_repo` (optional): comparator for remote settings/rules parity.
336: 
337: ### Outputs
338: 
339: - `delta_report.md`:
340:   - compliant items,
341:   - missing/misaligned items by severity,
342:   - commands planned/executed,
343:   - residual risks.
344: - `execution_log.json`:
345:   - actions attempted,
346:   - success/failure,
347:   - API response metadata.
348: - `check_results.md`:
349:   - lint/hook/test summary,
350:   - CI/ruleset/settings verification status.
351: 
352: ## Skill Workflow (Recommended)
353: 
354: ### Phase 1: Discover and classify
355: 
356: 1. Detect repository maturity:
357:    - docs-only vs active app code.
358: 2. Inventory existing baseline files:
359:    - governance docs
360:    - `.github/*`
361:    - quality hooks
362:    - release configuration.
363: 3. Query remote GitHub state (if enabled):
364:    - repo settings,
365:    - rulesets,
366:    - Actions permissions.
367: 
368: ### Phase 2: Compute delta
369: 
370: 1. Compare local files against `base-repo-template`.
371: 2. Compare remote state against:
372:    - template guidance expectations, and
373:    - known-good parity repo (if provided).
374: 3. Classify findings:
375:    - `critical`, `high`, `medium`, `low`.
376: 
377: ### Phase 3: Plan changes
378: 
379: 1. Generate an ordered remediation plan.
380: 2. Split local file edits and remote API updates into explicit steps.
381: 3. Require approval for high-impact operations (remote settings/rules updates).
382: 
383: ### Phase 4: Apply changes
384: 
385: 1. Apply file scaffolding/updates.
386: 2. Run local verification:
387:    - `pre-commit run --all-files`
388:    - markdown lint.
389: 3. Apply `gh api` updates for settings/rules (if approved/enabled).
390: 
391: ### Phase 5: Re-audit and report
392: 
393: 1. Re-run local checks.
394: 2. Re-query remote GitHub state.
395: 3. Produce final delta and residual risk report.
396: 
397: ## GitHub CLI Implementation Pattern for the Skill
398: 
399: Use this baseline pattern for all REST calls:
400: 
401: ```bash
402: gh api -X <METHOD> repos/<owner>/<repo>/<endpoint> \
403:   -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
404:   -H "X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28" \
405:   [--input payload.json | -F key=value ...]
406: ```
407: 
408: For complex nested payloads (for example, rulesets), prefer `--input <json-file>`.
409: 
410: ### Required remote operations the skill should support
411: 
412: 1. Repository settings patch:
413:    - merge strategy controls,
414:    - auto-merge/update-branch defaults,
415:    - wiki/issues toggles,
416:    - delete branch on merge.
417: 2. Ruleset create/update:
418:    - enforce checks and merge policy,
419:    - include integration bypass actors where required.
420: 3. Verification reads:
421:    - `repos/<owner>/<repo>`
422:    - `repos/<owner>/<repo>/rulesets`
423:    - `repos/<owner>/<repo>/actions/permissions`
424:    - `repos/<owner>/<repo>/actions/permissions/workflow`
425: 
426: ## Prompt and Guardrail Design for the Skill
427: 
428: ### Recommended system/developer prompt structure
429: 
430: 1. **Role and objective**
431: 2. **Allowed tools and when to use them**
432: 3. **Safety rules and forbidden actions**
433: 4. **Execution workflow**
434: 5. **Output contract**
435: 
436: ### Guardrails to enforce
437: 
438: - Never run destructive git commands unless explicitly requested.
439: - Never apply remote repo/ruleset changes without explicit approval when in apply mode.
440: - Never bypass quality hooks.
441: - Never assume org-level app availability; always verify and report.
442: - Never silently skip failed operations; include failures in final report.
443: 
444: ## Migration Plan: `base-repo-template` -> skill package
445: 
446: ### Step 1: Extract baseline spec from template repo
447: 
448: Create a normalized machine-readable baseline spec, for example:
449: 
450: - `baseline/files.json`
451: - `baseline/repo-settings.json`
452: - `baseline/ruleset.json`
453: - `baseline/checks.json`
454: 
455: Each item should include:
456: 
457: - required/optional flag,
458: - rationale,
459: - docs-first exceptions,
460: - severity if missing.
461: 
462: ### Step 2: Build skill definition
463: 
464: Create a skill folder with:
465: 
466: - `SKILL.md`:
467:   - purpose,
468:   - inputs/outputs,
469:   - workflow phases,
470:   - examples.
471: - optional helper artifacts:
472:   - command snippets,
473:   - checklists,
474:   - report templates.
475: 
476: ### Step 3: Implement two operating modes
477: 
478: - `new_repo` mode:
479:   - scaffold all required files and baseline configs.
480: - `upgrade_repo` mode:
481:   - detect drift and patch only required deltas.
482: 
483: ### Step 4: Add deterministic verification
484: 
485: - local:
486:   - pre-commit
487:   - markdownlint
488:   - repo diff summary
489: - remote:
490:   - settings parity
491:   - ruleset parity
492:   - workflow visibility and status checks parity.
493: 
494: ### Step 5: Add evaluation suite
495: 
496: Define regression fixtures:
497: 
498: - fixture A: empty docs-first repo
499: - fixture B: partially configured repo
500: - fixture C: mature repo with drift
501: 
502: For each fixture, assert:
503: 
504: - generated plan quality,
505: - applied changes correctness,
506: - idempotency on second run,
507: - no unsafe operations.
508: 
509: ## What the Future Skill Should Automate from This Conversation
510: 
511: - ingest template checklist and current target repo state
512: - apply baseline files and docs scaffolding
513: - configure pre-commit + CI + release scaffold
514: - run local checks and auto-fix formatting-only issues
515: - use `gh api` to enforce repo settings/rules parity
516: - compare against known-good repository profile
517: - output a final actionable parity report with residual gaps
518: 
519: ## Open Decisions to Encode as Skill Inputs
520: 
521: - whether to enforce strict parity with known-good settings (for example, wiki disabled)
522: - whether to enable additional security workflows (for example, CodeQL)
523: - whether to require integration bypass actors in rulesets by default
524: - whether to fail hard when semantic-release stamping targets are not configured in docs-first repos
525: 
526: ## Recommended Acceptance Criteria for "Reusable Skill Ready"
527: 
528: The skill is considered ready when:
529: 
530: 1. It can fully configure a new repo to baseline with no manual edits.
531: 2. It can upgrade a partially configured repo and produce a correct delta report.
532: 3. It can apply remote settings/rules safely with explicit confirmation.
533: 4. It is idempotent (second run produces no unexpected changes).
534: 5. It includes source-linked rationale and runbook-quality outputs for humans.
535: 
536: ## Addendum: Vendor-Specific Agent Skills Research (Skills-Focused)
537: 
538: This addendum narrows the prior research specifically to "agent skills" (or the vendor-equivalent concept when the exact term is not first-class).
539: 
540: ### Why this addendum exists
541: 
542: Some earlier references were broad "agent engineering" guidance. This section emphasizes official, skills-specific guidance from major vendors and clarifies terminology mismatches.
543: 
544: ## OpenAI (Official Skills Primitive)
545: 
546: Primary references:
547: 
548: - <https://developers.openai.com/codex/skills/>
549: - <https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/tools-skills/>
550: - <https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/skills_in_api/>
551: - <https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/tools/>
552: 
553: Skills-specific points to carry into our template-skill design:
554: 
555: - OpenAI explicitly defines skills as reusable, versioned bundles (instructions + scripts + assets) anchored by `SKILL.md`.
556: - Skills are intended to keep system prompts slim; reusable procedures should move into skills.
557: - Best practices explicitly call for:
558:   - single-purpose skills,
559:   - clear invocation descriptions,
560:   - imperative steps with explicit inputs/outputs,
561:   - avoiding duplicate logic in global prompts.
562: - Skill references can be attached programmatically (for example, shell tool environments with versioned skill references).
563: 
564: Implication for this migration:
565: 
566: - Treat `base-repo-template` conversion as a first-class Skills package with versioned releases and explicit trigger descriptions.
567: 
568: ## Anthropic (Official Agent Skills + Authoring Model)
569: 
570: Primary references:
571: 
572: - <https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/overview>
573: - <https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/best-practices>
574: - <https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills>
575: - <https://github.com/anthropics/skills>
576: 
577: Skills-specific points to carry into our template-skill design:
578: 
579: - Anthropic documents the progressive disclosure model explicitly:
580:   - metadata discovery first,
581:   - `SKILL.md` loaded on trigger,
582:   - references/resources loaded as needed.
583: - Authoring constraints and conventions are clear:
584:   - concise, high-signal `description` is critical for invocation quality,
585:   - keep `SKILL.md` focused (their guidance suggests keeping it manageable; commonly under 500 lines),
586:   - avoid deep nested references,
587:   - link support files directly from `SKILL.md`.
588: - Invocation control and tool restrictions can be configured in skill frontmatter (for Claude Code contexts).
589: 
590: Implication for this migration:
591: 
592: - The template-to-skill conversion should adopt a strict `SKILL.md` + `references/` structure with shallow reference depth and explicit trigger language.
593: 
594: ## Vercel (Skills Ecosystem + Packaging + Distribution)
595: 
596: Primary references:
597: 
598: - <https://vercel.com/blog/agent-skills-explained-an-faq>
599: - <https://vercel.com/kb/guide/agent-skills-creating-installing-and-sharing-reusable-agent-context>
600: - <https://vercel.com/docs/agent-resources/skills>
601: - <https://vercel.com/docs/agent-resources>
602: 
603: Skills-specific points to carry into our template-skill design:
604: 
605: - Vercel frames skills as packaged, reusable workflows (not merely prompts), and positions them as complementary to MCP servers, tools, and system prompts.
606: - Distribution/install patterns are operationalized through `skills` CLI (including selective installs, global installs, and list/search workflows).
607: - Emphasis on reusable "procedural knowledge packages" aligns with our target of encoding repeatable repo-setup runbooks.
608: 
609: Implication for this migration:
610: 
611: - Publish the converted template skill in a structure compatible with shared skills ecosystems and include installation/discovery metadata.
612: 
613: ## GitHub (Copilot Agent Skills + Adjacent Customization Layers)
614: 
615: Primary references:
616: 
617: - <https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/reference/customization-cheat-sheet>
618: - <https://docs.github.com/copilot/customizing-copilot/adding-custom-instructions-for-github-copilot>
619: - <https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/tutorials/customization-library/custom-instructions/your-first-custom-instructions>
620: - <https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/customization/overview>
621: 
622: Skills-specific points to carry into our template-skill design:
623: 
624: - GitHub differentiates:
625:   - custom instructions (always-on),
626:   - prompt files (manual one-shot templates),
627:   - custom agents (persona/tool profile),
628:   - agent skills (reusable capability package).
629: - Copilot customization docs explicitly include "Agent skills" as a separate feature category and scope model.
630: - Practical lesson: skills should hold reusable, multi-step workflows, while repo instructions hold baseline conventions.
631: 
632: Implication for this migration:
633: 
634: - Keep `copilot-instructions`/`AGENTS.md` minimal and move execution runbooks into skill packages.
635: 
636: ## Google (No First-Class "Agent Skills" Term in Core Gemini Docs)
637: 
638: Primary references:
639: 
640: - <https://google.github.io/adk-docs/>
641: - <https://google.github.io/adk-docs/get-started/about/>
642: - <https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/live-api/best-practices>
643: 
644: Important terminology note:
645: 
646: - Google's official docs currently center on ADK agents, tools, workflows, and modular composition rather than a first-class "Agent Skills" primitive branded the same way as OpenAI/Anthropic/Vercel/GitHub.
647: - Google guidance still maps cleanly to skills design principles:
648:   - modular reusable capabilities,
649:   - precise tool definitions and invocation conditions,
650:   - explicit system instruction ordering,
651:   - multi-step orchestration and evaluation.
652: 
653: Implication for this migration:
654: 
655: - For Google-compatible implementations, map "skill" to a modular ADK capability/workflow package with clear entry conditions and tool contracts.
656: 
657: ## Consolidated Skills Best Practices for This Project
658: 
659: Use these as hard requirements when converting `base-repo-template` to a reusable skill:
660: 
661: 1. **One skill, one coherent job**
662:    - Keep the baseline repo setup skill focused; split optional advanced operations into separate skills.
663: 2. **Strong trigger descriptions**
664:    - Description must state both "what this does" and "when to use it."
665: 3. **Progressive disclosure**
666:    - Keep `SKILL.md` concise; move heavy details into referenced files.
667: 4. **Shallow references**
668:    - Reference files directly from `SKILL.md`; avoid deep reference chains.
669: 5. **Executable determinism**
670:    - For automation steps, prefer deterministic scripts and explicit command sequences.
671: 6. **Separation of concerns**
672:    - Global behavior/rules in system/repo instructions; repeatable procedures in skills.
673: 7. **Versioned release discipline**
674:    - Treat skill bundles as versioned artifacts; pin versions in production workflows.
675: 8. **Safety and approval gates**
676:    - Add explicit approval checks before high-impact remote operations (`gh api` settings/rules updates).
677: 9. **Evaluation-backed rollout**
678:    - Validate skill behavior with fixture repos and idempotency checks.
679: 10. **Cross-platform mapping**
680:     - Preserve a provider-neutral core structure so the same workflow can be adapted across OpenAI, Anthropic, GitHub Copilot, and Google ADK-based implementations.

(End of file - total 680 lines)
</content>

# Forge Program Tracker Template Target State Handoff

## Purpose

This document captures the work completed to apply the `base-repo-template` standards to `forge-program-tracker` and establish the current ideal target state for future template-agent behavior.

## Repositories Involved

- Source template baseline:
  - `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
- Target repository:
  - `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
- Known-good comparator for settings/rules:
  - `https://github.com/liatrio-labs/open-source-project-template/`

## High-Level Outcome

`forge-program-tracker` was brought from a docs-only early state to a template-aligned baseline with:

- governance and collaboration docs/templates

parts.text:
**Structuring my opinion**

I need to express my opinion concisely, perhaps in a structured format. I want to mention both strengths and concerns, making sure to highlight that this is more of a design brief than a handoff. It does feel a bit lengthy, though. I think I should also reference the file path, but I don’t believe there's a need to ask about that. Let’s make sure my feedback is clear and to the point!

🤖🎵🔍
It’s strong on content, but it reads more like a combined handoff + design spec + research memo than a clean “target state handoff.”

- Biggest strength: it captures concrete implementation state well, especially the split between local repo changes and remote GitHub settings/rules work in `temp/template-target-state-handoff.md`.
- It also does a good job preserving operational knowledge that would otherwise get lost, especially the `gh api` patterns, verification steps, and the docs-first exceptions.
- The main weakness is scope creep: the document starts as a historical handoff, then turns into a future skill product spec, then turns into cross-vendor research. Those are all useful, but mixing them makes the core handoff harder to consume.
- At 680 lines, it is probably too long to serve as the primary “what happened / what is the target state” artifact. A future agent or human may miss the key decisions because they are buried under strategy material.
- I’d split it into 3 docs:
  1. `handoff.md` — what changed, current target state, caveats, exact repo/rules parity decisions
  2. `skill-requirements.md` — inputs, outputs, workflow, guardrails, acceptance criteria
  3. `vendor-research.md` — OpenAI/Anthropic/Vercel/GitHub/Google guidance and synthesis
- I’d also add a short “Target State Snapshot” section near the top with a checklist/table of the intended end state: required files, required hooks, required workflows, required remote settings, required ruleset properties. That would make it much more actionable.

My take: excellent raw material, but it wants editing for purpose clarity. Right now it is valuable, just not optimized for fast reuse. If you want, I can turn it into a tighter handoff package with the split above.

# Skill Builder/Auditor

## Purpose

This skill supports two workflows:

1. `create_skill`: create a new skill package from a user request.
2. `audit_skill`: audit an existing skill package against current best practices.

Outputs must be vendor-neutral by default, grounded in the AGENTS.md ecosystem and portable across agent platforms.

## Mode Selection

Choose mode based on user intent:

- If user asks to build, draft, generate, or scaffold a skill: use `create_skill`.
- If user asks to review, assess, score, modernize, or validate a skill: use `audit_skill`.

If intent is ambiguous, do not proceed directly to generation. Use a clarification step first.

## Required Clarification and Planning

Before generating any skill artifacts:

1. Confirm objective and scope.
2. Confirm target repository/path.
3. Confirm whether user wants:
   - minimal output (MVP), or
   - full package (references/templates/checklists).
4. Confirm constraints:
   - vendor neutrality expectations,
   - safety/approval boundaries for remote operations.
5. Propose a concise plan and get acceptance.

Do not skip clarification for ambiguous requests.

## Output Contract

### For `create_skill`

Produce:

1. Skill specification summary:
   - purpose,
   - trigger conditions,
   - scope boundaries,
   - modes/workflows.
2. File plan:
   - exact files/folders to create,
   - short rationale per file.
3. Generated skill package files:
   - `SKILL.md`,
   - references,
   - templates,
   - checklists.
4. Validation results:
   - markdown lint pass/fail,
   - known gaps and follow-ups.

### For `audit_skill`

Produce:

1. Audit report with severity levels:
   - `critical`, `high`, `medium`, `low`.
2. Evidence:
   - concrete file references and snippets.
3. Remediation plan:
   - ordered fixes,
   - which are blocking vs non-blocking.
4. Updated compliance scorecard:
   - by category (structure, instructions, references, safety, portability, maintainability).

## Vendor-Neutral Baseline

Use AGENTS.md-aligned principles as the default baseline:

- concise and explicit instructions,
- clear operational commands,
- minimal ambiguity,
- deterministic outputs where possible.

Vendor-specific conventions are allowed as optional adapter guidance, not as the required output format.

## Research and Freshness Rules

When needed, perform updated research before final recommendations:

- prioritize official docs,
- compare across major vendors,
- note conflicts and choose neutral guidance by default.

Use `references/research-playbook.md` for trigger conditions and source priority.

## Safety Rules

- Never apply destructive operations without explicit user request.
- Never assume a platform-specific skill format as mandatory when user requested vendor-neutral output.
- Never represent optional vendor guidance as universal requirement.
- Clearly label assumptions and unresolved questions.

## Working Files

Use these companion files:

- `references/agentsmd-baseline.md`
- `references/vendor-adapters.md`
- `references/research-playbook.md`
- `templates/new-skill-spec-template.md`
- `templates/skill-audit-report-template.md`
- `checklists/authoring-checklist.md`
- `checklists/audit-checklist.md`

<content>1: # AGENTS.md Baseline for Skill Design
2: 
3: ## Objective
4: 
5: Define a vendor-neutral baseline for skill creation and auditing using AGENTS.md-style principles.
6: 
7: ## Core AGENTS.md Concepts
8: 
9: - AGENTS.md is a predictable place for agent-facing guidance, similar to a README for agents.
10: - Guidance should be explicit, practical, and scoped to project workflows.
11: - Agents should get clear execution instructions (commands, checks, conventions) with minimal ambiguity.
12: 
13: ## Baseline Requirements for Skills
14: 
15: Every generated or audited skill should satisfy:
16: 
17: 1. Clear purpose statement
18: 2. Explicit trigger conditions (when to use the skill)
19: 3. Bounded scope (what the skill should not do)
20: 4. Stepwise workflow instructions
21: 5. Deterministic output contract
22: 6. Safety/approval boundaries
23: 7. Reference links to deeper guidance
24: 
25: ## Recommended Skill Structure
26: 
27: ```text
28: skill-name/
29: ├── SKILL.md
30: ├── references/
31: │   ├── domain-context.md
32: │   └── research-playbook.md
33: ├── templates/
34: │   └── output-template.md
35: └── checklists/
36:     └── quality-checklist.md
37: ```
38: 
39: ## Instruction Writing Rules
40: 
41: - Use imperative language ("Do X", "Then do Y").
42: - Name explicit inputs and outputs for each step.
43: - Avoid vague language ("as needed", "if appropriate") unless conditions are defined.
44: - Keep global behavior separate from task-specific workflows.
45: 
46: ## What Belongs in AGENTS.md vs Skills
47: 
48: Use AGENTS.md for:
49: 
50: - repository-wide standing guidance,
51: - toolchain commands and validation gates,
52: - broad collaboration norms.
53: 
54: Use skills for:
55: 
56: - reusable, named workflows,
57: - procedural logic with branching,
58: - packaged templates/checklists/reference context.
59: 
60: ## Quality Signals
61: 
62: High-quality skills typically include:
63: 
64: - clarification-first behavior for ambiguous requests,
65: - explicit severity model for audits,
66: - direct references (not deeply nested references),
67: - reproducible output formats.

(End of file - total 67 lines)
</content>

# AGENTS.md Baseline for Skill Design

## Objective

Define a vendor-neutral baseline for skill creation and auditing using AGENTS.md-style principles.

## Core AGENTS.md Concepts

- AGENTS.md is a predictable place for agent-facing guidance, similar to a README for agents.
- Guidance should be explicit, practical, and scoped to project workflows.
- Agents should get clear execution instructions (commands, checks, conventions) with minimal ambiguity.

## Baseline Requirements for Skills

Every generated or audited skill should satisfy:

1. Clear purpose statement
2. Explicit trigger conditions (when to use the skill)
3. Bounded scope (what the skill should not do)
4. Stepwise workflow instructions

<content>1: # Vendor Adapter Guidance (Optional Layer)
2: 
3: ## Purpose
4: 
5: Keep skill outputs vendor-neutral by default, but provide adapter mappings for common platforms.
6: 
7: ## Rule of Use
8: 
9: - Default deliverable: vendor-neutral.
10: - Vendor-specific adaptations: only when user requests or implementation environment requires it.
11: - Never treat one vendor's format as universally required.
12: 
13: ## OpenAI Adapter
14: 
15: Relevant concepts:
16: 
17: - reusable skill bundles with `SKILL.md`,
18: - versioned skill assets and references,
19: - hosted/local shell attachment patterns.
20: 
21: Mapping guidance:
22: 
23: - keep neutral `SKILL.md` content unchanged,
24: - add OpenAI-specific packaging metadata only in an adapter layer,
25: - preserve output contract and safety rules.
26: 
27: ## Anthropic/Claude Adapter
28: 
29: Relevant concepts:
30: 
31: - progressive disclosure model (metadata, then `SKILL.md`, then references),
32: - practical guidance to keep `SKILL.md` focused and references direct,
33: - optional frontmatter fields for invocation/tool restrictions in Claude Code contexts.
34: 
35: Mapping guidance:
36: 
37: - maintain concise `SKILL.md`,
38: - link support files directly from `SKILL.md`,
39: - add Claude-specific fields only when targeting Claude runtime.
40: 
41: ## GitHub Copilot Adapter
42: 
43: Relevant distinctions:
44: 
45: - custom instructions (always-on),
46: - prompt files (manual one-shot),
47: - custom agents (persona/tool profile),
48: - skills (reusable capability package).
49: 
50: Mapping guidance:
51: 
52: - keep reusable multi-step workflows in skills,
53: - keep repo-wide norms in instructions files,
54: - avoid duplicating workflow logic across both.
55: 
56: ## Vercel/Skills Ecosystem Adapter
57: 
58: Relevant concepts:
59: 
60: - skills as reusable procedural knowledge packages,
61: - install and distribution via skills tooling,
62: - portability across multiple agent surfaces.
63: 
64: Mapping guidance:
65: 
66: - package the skill for discoverability/installability,
67: - preserve neutral content and add distribution metadata separately.
68: 
69: ## Google ADK-Compatible Adapter
70: 
71: Terminology note:
72: 
73: - Google docs emphasize modular agents/tools/workflows rather than a first-class "skills" primitive with the same branding.
74: 
75: Mapping guidance:
76: 
77: - map neutral skill workflows to reusable ADK modules and tool orchestration flows,
78: - preserve entry conditions, safety boundaries, and output contracts.
79: 
80: ## Cross-Vendor Portability Checklist
81: 
82: Before publishing vendor-adapted outputs:
83: 
84: 1. Neutral baseline still exists as canonical source.
85: 2. Adapter adds metadata, not behavior drift.
86: 3. Trigger language remains clear and deterministic.
87: 4. Safety boundaries are preserved.
88: 5. Audit rubric and severity levels remain unchanged.

(End of file - total 88 lines)
</content>

<type>file</type>
<content>1: # Research Playbook for Skills Best Practices
2: 
3: ## Purpose
4: 
5: Define when and how to refresh skill guidance using current external sources while preserving a stable vendor-neutral baseline.
6: 
7: ## When to Trigger Fresh Research
8: 
9: Run updated research when any of these occur:
10: 
11: 1. New model/runtime rollout or major vendor feature updates
12: 2. Skill audit failures caused by outdated assumptions
13: 3. User request for "latest best practices"
14: 4. Migration to a new agent platform
15: 5. Security/process incident tied to skill behavior
16: 
17: ## Source Priority Order
18: 
19: Use this strict priority order:
20: 
21: 1. Official vendor documentation (primary)
22: 2. Official vendor SDK/API references
23: 3. Vendor-maintained examples/cookbooks
24: 4. Reputable ecosystem material (secondary, clearly labeled)
25: 
26: Do not use community commentary as primary evidence when official docs exist.
27: 
28: ## Required Vendors for Cross-Check
29: 
30: For broad best-practice updates, check:
31: 
32: - OpenAI
33: - Anthropic
34: - Vercel
35: - GitHub
36: - Google
37: 
38: If one vendor has no first-class "skill" primitive, map to closest official modular agent capability and note terminology differences.
39: 
40: ## Research Procedure
41: 
42: 1. Define research question in one sentence.
43: 2. Gather official docs from each vendor.
44: 3. Extract only operational guidance:
45:    - packaging,
46:    - triggers,
47:    - safety/guardrails,
48:    - tool invocation,
49:    - evaluation/testing.
50: 4. Build a comparison matrix.
51: 5. Resolve conflicts with neutral-first policy:
52:    - preserve neutral baseline,
53:    - move vendor-specific behavior to adapters.
54: 6. Update references/templates/checklists.
55: 7. Record date and source list in the output report.
56: 
57: ## Conflict Resolution Rules
58: 
59: When vendor guidance differs:
60: 
61: 1. Prefer guidance shared by multiple vendors.
62: 2. If unique vendor behavior exists, keep it in `vendor-adapters.md`.
63: 3. Do not break neutral output contract to satisfy one vendor.
64: 4. Explicitly document trade-offs.
65: 
66: ## Citation Requirements in Generated Outputs
67: 
68: Every research-backed recommendation must include:
69: 
70: - source URL,
71: - source type (`official-doc`, `official-sdk`, `official-example`, `secondary`),
72: - short rationale sentence.
73: 
74: Example:
75: 
76: ```text
77: [official-doc] https://developers.openai.com/codex/skills/ - Defines skill bundles and best-practice trigger guidance.
78: ```
79: 
80: ## Freshness and Review Cadence
81: 
82: - lightweight review: monthly
83: - full cross-vendor refresh: quarterly
84: - immediate refresh: after major platform changelog events
85: 
86: ## Definition of Done for Research Update
87: 
88: - all five vendor sections reviewed or explicitly marked not applicable
89: - neutral baseline unchanged or improved
90: - adapter guidance updated where needed
91: - templates/checklists updated to reflect new findings
92: - output includes updated citation list and change summary

(End of file - total 92 lines)
</content>
