<path>/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker/.github/workflows/ruleset-sync.yml</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>1: name: Sync Ruleset Config
2: 
3: on:
4:   workflow_dispatch:
5: 
6: permissions:
7:   contents: read
8: 
9: jobs:
10:   sync:
11:     name: Apply Ruleset Desired State
12:     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13:     env:
14:       GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
15:     steps:
16:       - name: Checkout repository
17:         uses: actions/checkout@v6.0.1
18: 
19:       - name: Validate desired ruleset config
20:         run: jq -e . .github/ruleset-config.json > /dev/null
21: 
22:       - name: Build effective ruleset config
23:         run: |
24:           set -euo pipefail
25: 
26:           octo_sts_id="$(gh api apps/octo-sts --jq '.id')"
27:           jq --argjson octo_sts_id "${octo_sts_id}" '
28:             .bypass_actors = (
29:               (.bypass_actors // [])
30:               + [
31:                 {
32:                   actor_type: "Integration",
33:                   actor_id: $octo_sts_id,
34:                   bypass_mode: "always"
35:                 }
36:               ]
37:               | unique_by(.actor_type, .actor_id)
38:             )
39:           ' .github/ruleset-config.json > effective-ruleset-config.json
40: 
41:           jq -e . effective-ruleset-config.json > /dev/null
42: 
43:       - name: Resolve target ruleset ID
44:         id: resolve
45:         run: |
46:           set -euo pipefail
47:           ruleset_name="$(jq -r '.name' effective-ruleset-config.json)"
48:           ruleset_target="$(jq -r '.target' effective-ruleset-config.json)"
49: 
50:           ruleset_id="$(
51:             gh api "repos/${{ github.repository }}/rulesets" \
52:               --jq ".[] | select(.name == \"$ruleset_name\" and .target == \"$ruleset_target\") | .id" \
53:               | head -n 1
54:           )"
55: 
56:           echo "ruleset_id=${ruleset_id}" >> "${GITHUB_OUTPUT}"
57: 
58:       - name: Create or update ruleset desired state
59:         run: |
60:           set -euo pipefail
61:           ruleset_id="${{ steps.resolve.outputs.ruleset_id }}"
62: 
63:           if [[ -n "${ruleset_id}" ]]; then
64:             gh api \
65:               --method PUT \
66:               -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
67:               -H "X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28" \
68:               "repos/${{ github.repository }}/rulesets/${ruleset_id}" \
69:               --input effective-ruleset-config.json > /dev/null
70:             echo "Updated ruleset ${ruleset_id}."
71:           else
72:             gh api \
73:               --method POST \
74:               -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
75:               -H "X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2022-11-28" \
76:               "repos/${{ github.repository }}/rulesets" \
77:               --input effective-ruleset-config.json > /dev/null
78:             echo "Created new ruleset from desired state."
79:           fi
80: 
81:       - name: Resolve ruleset ID after apply
82:         id: resolve_after
83:         run: |
84:           set -euo pipefail
85:           ruleset_name="$(jq -r '.name' effective-ruleset-config.json)"
86:           ruleset_target="$(jq -r '.target' effective-ruleset-config.json)"
87:           ruleset_id="$(
88:             gh api "repos/${{ github.repository }}/rulesets" \
89:               --jq ".[] | select(.name == \"$ruleset_name\" and .target == \"$ruleset_target\") | .id" \
90:               | head -n 1
91:           )"
92: 
93:           if [[ -z "${ruleset_id}" ]]; then
94:             echo "Failed to resolve ruleset after apply."
95:             exit 1
96:           fi
97: 
98:           echo "ruleset_id=${ruleset_id}" >> "${GITHUB_OUTPUT}"
99: 
100:       - name: Verify sync parity
101:         run: |
102:           set -euo pipefail
103: 
104:           # Ruleset API responses can reorder rules and omit defaulted fields.
105:           # Normalize known defaults so parity checks compare semantic state.
106:           #
107:           # Keep bypass_actors in sync parity checks here because this workflow
108:           # runs with elevated permissions and should enforce full desired state.
109:           normalize_filter='
110:             def normalize_rule:
111:               if .type == "required_status_checks" then
112:                 .parameters = ((.parameters // {}) + {do_not_enforce_on_create: (.parameters.do_not_enforce_on_create // false)})
113:               elif .type == "pull_request" then
114:                 .parameters = ((.parameters // {}) + {required_reviewers: (.parameters.required_reviewers // [])})
115:               else
116:                 .
117:               end;
118:             def normalize:
119:               {
120:                 name,
121:                 target,
122:                 enforcement,
123:                 conditions,
124:                 rules: ((.rules // []) | map(normalize_rule) | sort_by(.type)),
125:                 bypass_actors: ((.bypass_actors // []) | sort_by(.actor_type, .actor_id, .bypass_mode))
126:               };
127:             normalize
128:           '
129: 
130:           jq -S "${normalize_filter}" effective-ruleset-config.json > desired_ruleset.json
131:           gh api "repos/${{ github.repository }}/rulesets/${{ steps.resolve_after.outputs.ruleset_id }}" \
132:             | jq -S "${normalize_filter}" > live_ruleset.json
133: 
134:           diff -u desired_ruleset.json live_ruleset.json
135:           echo "Ruleset successfully synced."

(End of file - total 135 lines)
</content>

on:

jobs:

    steps:

<type>file</type>
<content>1: // Entire CLI plugin for OpenCode
2: // Auto-generated by `entire enable --agent opencode`
3: // Do not edit manually — changes will be overwritten on next install.
4: // Requires Bun runtime (used by OpenCode's plugin system for loading ESM plugins).
5: import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
6: 
7: export const EntirePlugin: Plugin = async ({ $, directory }) => {
8:   const ENTIRE_CMD = "entire"
9:   // Track seen user messages to fire turn-start only once per message
10:   const seenUserMessages = new Set<string>()
11:   // Track current session ID for message events (which don't include sessionID)
12:   let currentSessionID: string | null = null
13:   // In-memory store for message metadata (role, tokens, etc.)
14:   const messageStore = new Map<string, any>()
15: 
16:   /**
17:    * Pipe JSON payload to an entire hooks command (async).
18:    * Errors are logged but never thrown — plugin failures must not crash OpenCode.
19:    */
20:   async function callHook(hookName: string, payload: Record<string, unknown>) {
21:     try {
22:       const json = JSON.stringify(payload)
23:       await $`echo ${json} | ${ENTIRE_CMD} hooks opencode ${hookName}`.quiet().nothrow()
24:     } catch {
25:       // Silently ignore — plugin failures must not crash OpenCode
26:     }
27:   }
28: 
29:   /**
30:    * Synchronous variant for hooks that fire near process exit (turn-end, session-end).
31:    * `opencode run` breaks its event loop on the same session.status idle event that
32:    * triggers turn-end. The async callHook would be killed before completing.
33:    * Bun.spawnSync blocks the event loop, preventing exit until the hook finishes.
34:    */
35:   function callHookSync(hookName: string, payload: Record<string, unknown>) {
36:     try {
37:       const json = JSON.stringify(payload)
38:       Bun.spawnSync(["sh", "-c", `${ENTIRE_CMD} hooks opencode ${hookName}`], {
39:         cwd: directory,
40:         stdin: new TextEncoder().encode(json + "\n"),
41:         stdout: "ignore",
42:         stderr: "ignore",
43:       })
44:     } catch {
45:       // Silently ignore — plugin failures must not crash OpenCode
46:     }
47:   }
48: 
49:   return {
50:     event: async ({ event }) => {
51:       switch (event.type) {
52:         case "session.created": {
53:           const session = (event as any).properties?.info
54:           if (!session?.id) break
55:           // Reset per-session tracking state when switching sessions.
56:           if (currentSessionID !== session.id) {
57:             seenUserMessages.clear()
58:             messageStore.clear()
59:           }
60:           currentSessionID = session.id
61:           await callHook("session-start", {
62:             session_id: session.id,
63:           })
64:           break
65:         }
66: 
67:         case "message.updated": {
68:           const msg = (event as any).properties?.info
69:           if (!msg) break
70:           // Store message metadata (role, time, tokens, etc.)
71:           messageStore.set(msg.id, msg)
72:           break
73:         }
74: 
75:         case "message.part.updated": {
76:           const part = (event as any).properties?.part
77:           if (!part?.messageID) break
78: 
79:           // Fire turn-start on the first text part of a new user message
80:           const msg = messageStore.get(part.messageID)
81:           if (msg?.role === "user" && part.type === "text" && !seenUserMessages.has(msg.id)) {
82:             seenUserMessages.add(msg.id)
83:             const sessionID = msg.sessionID ?? currentSessionID
84:             if (sessionID) {
85:               await callHook("turn-start", {
86:                 session_id: sessionID,
87:                 prompt: part.text ?? "",
88:               })
89:             }
90:           }
91:           break
92:         }
93: 
94:         case "session.status": {
95:           // session.status fires in both TUI and non-interactive (run) mode.
96:           // session.idle is deprecated and not reliably emitted in run mode.
97:           const props = (event as any).properties
98:           if (props?.status?.type !== "idle") break
99:           const sessionID = props?.sessionID
100:           if (!sessionID) break
101:           // Use sync variant: `opencode run` exits on the same idle event,
102:           // so an async hook would be killed before completing.
103:           callHookSync("turn-end", {
104:             session_id: sessionID,
105:           })
106:           break
107:         }
108: 
109:         case "session.compacted": {
110:           const sessionID = (event as any).properties?.sessionID
111:           if (!sessionID) break
112:           await callHook("compaction", {
113:             session_id: sessionID,
114:           })
115:           break
116:         }
117: 
118:         case "session.deleted": {
119:           const session = (event as any).properties?.info
120:           if (!session?.id) break
121:           seenUserMessages.clear()
122:           messageStore.clear()
123:           currentSessionID = null
124:           // Use sync variant: session-end may fire during shutdown.
125:           callHookSync("session-end", {
126:             session_id: session.id,
127:           })
128:           break
129:         }
130:       }
131:     },
132:   }
133: }

(End of file - total 133 lines)
</content>

**Exploring codebase insights**

I'm considering whether to explore an agent for a quick second opinion, which could assist in comparing details. However, it may not be necessary. I might inspect the discrepancy between documentation naming conventions in `forge-program-tracker/docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` and the README, noting the uppercase and lowercase use. The user is looking for my understanding of the repo's governance and structure, so I should present a thoughtful synthesis about its strengths and implications for skill development.

**Identifying discrepancies and details**

I'm thinking about mentioning a few gaps in the base CONTRIBUTING document, like how it allows `--no-verify` while the target repo has removed that feature. There's also an inconsistency in file casing between `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` and `docs/development.md`. It's important to note that only the forge has GitHub-specific workflows. I might need to clarify with the user if they require more information. Since they asked for a review, I should keep active markers starting with 🤖🎵🔍 and ensure my response is structured concisely, aiming for 700-900 words.

🤖🎵🔍
I reviewed both repos, and I’m much clearer now on what “good” looks like for this skill.

**Bottom line**
- `base-repo-template` is a solid generic baseline for repo governance and delivery hygiene.
- `forge-program-tracker` is the more important signal for the new skill because it shows how that baseline was adapted into an AI-native, docs-first, real-world target state.
- So for the skill, I would treat `base-repo-template` as the source baseline and `forge-program-tracker` as the stronger definition of the desired operating model.

**What `base-repo-template` gets right**
- It establishes the core scaffolding: `README.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, `.releaserc.toml`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `.github/workflows/release.yml`, `.github/ruleset-config.json`, issue templates, PR template, and `docs/template-guide.md`.
- It encodes good default quality gates in `.pre-commit-config.yaml`: YAML/TOML checks, markdown linting, commitlint, gitleaks, and Renovate validation.
- It treats repo governance as code, especially through `.github/ruleset-config.json`, `CODEOWNERS`, and release automation.
- It includes AI-awareness, but lightly: `AGENTS.md` is present and useful, though still generic.

**Where `base-repo-template` is still “template-ish” rather than target-state**
- It has lots of placeholders and manual follow-up steps in `docs/template-guide.md`.
- It expects humans to finish remote GitHub setup rather than making remote parity part of the system.
- `CONTRIBUTING.md` still contains guidance to skip hooks with `--no-verify`, which is exactly the kind of thing an AI-native bootstrap should discourage or remove.
- The CI is intentionally generic, but that means it is not yet optimized for docs-first repos or AI-assistant-heavy workflows.

**What `forge-program-tracker` adds that feels genuinely AI-native**
- It turns a generic baseline into a repo that is ready for AI agents to work safely and productively even before app code exists.
- The docs are purpose-built, not placeholder-driven:
  - `README.md` is concrete about stack direction and current maturity.
  - `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` is actionable and repo-specific.
  - `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and `docs/PRD.md` give an AI enough product and system context to reason usefully.
  - `AGENTS.md` is concise and operational rather than boilerplate.
- It adds a clean docs-first adaptation:
  - `ci.yml` keeps required checks alive with a placeholder `Run Tests` gate instead of pretending app tests exist yet.
  - `.releaserc.toml` is scaffolded without fake version stamping targets.
  - `.pre-commit-config.yaml` is ready now, with language-specific hooks deferred until implementation.
- It introduces feedback-loop automation around repo policy:
  - `ruleset-config.json` is treated as desired state.
  - `ruleset-sync.yml` can apply it.
  - `ci.yml` includes `Detect Ruleset Drift` to compare desired vs live state.
- That is a big deal: it shifts from “repo bootstrap” to “repo stays in a known-good state.”

**What feels most important for the new skill**
- An AI-native repo template is not just files; it is a set of enforceable loops:
  - local guardrails,
  - CI guardrails,
  - release guardrails,
  - remote GitHub policy guardrails,
  - agent-operability context.
- The skill should therefore optimize for:
  - deterministic baseline setup,
  - explicit maturity-aware adaptations,
  - approval-gated remote operations,
  - re-audit/report after apply,
  - idempotency.

**The strongest target-state signals from `forge-program-tracker`**
- `AGENTS.md` is focused on how an AI should behave in the repo, not just what files exist.
- `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` includes repo policy sync patterns, not only local setup.
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` preserves required status checks while the repo is still docs-first.
- `.github/workflows/ruleset-sync.yml` plus `.github/ruleset-config.json` creates an apply-and-verify loop for branch governance.
- `.env.example` documents integration surfaces early, which helps both humans and agents reason about future system shape.
- Tooling files like `.claude/settings.json`, `.cursor/hooks.json`, and `.opencode/plugins/entire.ts` show another dimension of AI-native readiness: observability and workflow instrumentation for agent sessions.

**My updated view of “good”**
A good AI-native repo template should produce a repo that:
- gives agents enough context to act correctly,
- constrains them with meaningful guardrails,
- preserves quality through hooks and CI,
- treats remote policy as part of the system,
- supports docs-first repos without pretending implementation exists,
- is ready to evolve into a code repo without redoing the foundation.

**What I’d carry into `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`**
- Use `base-repo-template` for baseline inventory and scaffolding patterns.
- Use `forge-program-tracker` as the canonical example of:
  - docs-first maturity adaptations,
  - AI-operability context,
  - ruleset drift detection/sync,
  - approval-gated GitHub remote management,
  - practical repo-specific hardening beyond placeholders.

**One important nuance**
- Repo contents alone do not capture everything in the handoff, especially remote GitHub settings parity done via `gh api`.
- So the skill should explicitly distinguish:
  1. file-level baseline,
  2. remote GitHub desired state,
  3. maturity-based exceptions.

Net: I’m confident the skill should be modeled more on the *delta between these two repos* than on the base template alone. That delta is where the real AI-native template behavior lives.

If you want, next I can turn this into a concrete “good looks like” rubric for the skill: categories, required artifacts, optional artifacts, and maturity-based exceptions.
do it

**Planning a structured rubric**

I need to focus on creating a plan without making any edits right now. The user specifically refers to a "good looks rubric," so I'll provide that. I could consider updating any to-do items, but I wonder if it’s really necessary since Todowrite doesn’t modify files. I’ll activate the start markers and mention my plan, organizing it into sections. I’ll ensure it includes categories, required artifacts, optional artifacts, maturity-based exceptions, acceptance criteria, and skill implications. No changes will be made, just a structured rubric!

🤖🎵🔍
Here’s the “good looks like” rubric I’d use for `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.

**AI-Native Repo Rubric**

**1. Repo Intent Is Legible**
- Required:
  - `README.md` explains what the repo is, current maturity, stack direction, and how to get started.
  - `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` captures current or target system shape.
  - `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` gives runnable local quality commands.
  - `AGENTS.md` tells an AI how to operate safely and effectively in the repo.
- Good looks like:
  - no unresolved placeholders for identity, workflow, or ownership
  - docs reflect current reality, not generic template language
  - an AI can infer repo state, priorities, and guardrails without guessing

**2. Governance Is Standardized Early**
- Required:
  - `CONTRIBUTING.md`
  - `.github/pull_request_template.md`
  - `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*`
  - `.github/CODEOWNERS`
- Good looks like:
  - PR template asks for why, change summary, and validation
  - issue templates gather actionable inputs
  - ownership is concrete, not placeholder-only
  - contribution guidance matches actual repo gates

**3. Local Feedback Loops Exist**
- Required:
  - `.pre-commit-config.yaml`
  - `.markdownlint.yaml`
  - `.gitignore`
- Required baseline checks:
  - YAML/TOML validation
  - EOF/trailing whitespace/large file checks
  - markdown lint/fix
  - Conventional Commit enforcement
  - secret scanning
  - Renovate config validation if Renovate is present
- Good looks like:
  - checks are tech-agnostic first
  - language-specific hooks are added only when implementation warrants them
  - developers and agents can run `pre-commit run --all-files` as a single baseline verification step

**4. CI Preserves Quality Continuously**
- Required:
  - `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- Good looks like:
  - required status checks exist even in docs-first repos
  - CI mirrors local guardrails instead of inventing a separate standard
  - workflow is maturity-aware:
    - docs-first: placeholder or documentation gate for tests
    - implementation phase: real lint/test/build commands
- Stronger target state:
  - rules/policy drift is also checked in CI, not only code quality

**5. Release Path Is Pre-Wired**
- Required:
  - `.github/workflows/release.yml`
  - `.releaserc.toml`
- Good looks like:
  - Conventional Commits drive semantic versioning
  - release automation is scaffolded before product maturity requires it
  - config is honest about current state:
    - docs-first repos can leave `version_variables = []` and `assets = []`
    - implementation repos must configure real stamping/build assets
- Stronger target state:
  - release auth model is documented and reproducible

**6. Remote GitHub Policy Is Treated As Code**
- Required:
  - `.github/ruleset-config.json`
- Recommended:
  - ruleset sync workflow
  - CI drift detection against live GitHub ruleset state
- Good looks like:
  - desired branch protection is versioned in repo
  - merge policy, status checks, and bypass actors are explicit
  - repo settings/rules are auditable and reproducible via `gh api`
- This is one of the clearest signals of “AI-native” maturity because it closes the loop between declared and actual repo policy.

**7. AI Operability Is Explicit**
- Required:
  - `AGENTS.md`
- Recommended:
  - agent/tool-specific config files when actually used (`.claude/`, `.cursor/`, `.opencode/`, etc.)
- Good looks like:
  - AI instructions are concise, operational, and repo-specific
  - quality commands are easy to find
  - repo state and expectations are clear
  - secrets/placeholders are documented safely
- Stronger target state:
  - agent workflow instrumentation or hooks exist where useful, but they are additive, not required

**8. Environment and Integration Surfaces Are Documented**
- Required when integrations are expected:
  - `.env.example`
- Good looks like:
  - env vars are placeholders only, never live secrets
  - integration boundaries are visible early
  - docs-first repos can document future integrations without pretending they are fully implemented

**9. Maturity-Based Exceptions Are Intentional**
- Docs-first repo acceptable exceptions:
  - placeholder test job in CI
  - empty semantic-release stamp targets
  - commented language-specific hooks
  - architecture doc focused on target state rather than implemented state
- Not acceptable:
  - broken placeholders left unresolved
  - generic template docs that contradict repo reality
  - missing required status checks because “code doesn’t exist yet”
  - remote settings left as tribal knowledge

**10. Acceptance Criteria For “AI-Native Ready”**
- Required minimum:
  - repo purpose and maturity are explicit
  - governance docs/templates exist and are repo-specific
  - local quality gates run clean
  - CI enforces baseline checks
  - release scaffold exists
  - branch/ruleset policy is declared in repo
  - AI operating instructions exist
- Strong target state:
  - remote GitHub state can be inspected, compared, and reconciled
  - drift detection exists for repo policy
  - docs-first repos still preserve required review/status contracts
  - second-pass audit produces little or no unexpected delta

**Required Artifacts For The Skill**
- `README.md`
- `CONTRIBUTING.md`
- `AGENTS.md`
- `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`
- `.pre-commit-config.yaml`
- `.markdownlint.yaml`
- `.gitignore`
- `.github/pull_request_template.md`
- `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*`
- `.github/CODEOWNERS`
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- `.github/workflows/release.yml`
- `.releaserc.toml`
- `.github/ruleset-config.json`

**Optional Or Conditional Artifacts**
- `.env.example`
- `.github/renovate.json`
- `.github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml`
- ruleset sync workflow
- agent runtime configs like `.claude/`, `.cursor/`, `.opencode/`
- language-specific hook/workflow additions
- repo-specific planning/product docs like `docs/PRD.md`

**How This Should Shape The Skill**
- The skill should not just scaffold files.
- It should classify repo maturity, apply the right baseline, preserve docs-first exceptions, and optionally audit/apply remote GitHub parity.
- The primary output should be a delta against this rubric, not a blind copy from `base-repo-template`.

If you want, next I can convert this rubric into the actual skill contract: trigger phrases, workflows, file plan, and approval boundaries for `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
do it

**Considering instructions and clarification**

I’m thinking about whether I need to use a question tool for clarification. It seems we’re not blocked, so I don’t think it’s necessary to ask for opinions or input on trade-offs right now. Maybe I can include one open choice for the user later. Regarding the plan, I should mention one decision I'd like confirmed. There's some ambiguity around the scope that could affect the result, but I might be able to choose a reasonable default. I need to update the to-do list soon!

parts.text:
🤖🎵🔍
Here’s the concrete skill contract I’d use for `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.

**Skill Definition**
- Name: `ai-native-repo-bootstrap`
- Purpose: bootstrap or upgrade a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern devex guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.
- Positioning: “Get a target repo ready for AI-native development.”
- Core promise: not just file scaffolding; it establishes and verifies the operating model.

**When It Should Trigger**
- “bootstrap this repo for AI-native development”
- “prepare this repo so AI agents can work well”
- “upgrade this repo to modern devex standards”
- “bring this repo up to baseline”
- “set up repo guardrails / feedback loops / governance”
- “make this repo AI-ready”
- “align this repo with template baseline”

**When It Should Not Trigger**
- app feature implementation
- generic lint/config cleanup unrelated to repo bootstrap
- one-off CI fix without broader repo-baseline intent
- pure GitHub admin tasks with no repo baseline/adoption goal

**Scope Boundaries**
- In scope:
  - repo baseline audit
  - docs/governance scaffolding
  - local quality gates
  - CI/release scaffolding
  - ruleset/repo settings audit and optional apply planning
  - maturity-aware adaptation for docs-first repos
  - final delta/report output
- Out of scope:
  - business feature development
  - org-wide policy changes
  - secrets provisioning
  - destructive git cleanup
  - silently applying remote GitHub changes

**Workflows**
- `new_repo`
  1. inspect repo maturity and existing files
  2. scaffold required baseline artifacts
  3. adapt placeholders to repo identity and maturity
  4. run local verification checks
  5. audit remote GitHub state if enabled
  6. propose remote parity changes and require approval before apply
  7. emit final readiness report
- `upgrade_repo`
  1. inventory current local and remote state
  2. compute delta against baseline rubric
  3. classify findings by severity
  4. apply missing local baseline changes
  5. verify locally
  6. audit remote GitHub parity and require approval before apply
  7. emit delta/residual-risk report

**Execution Policy**
- Default behavior:
  - audit local state
  - audit remote GitHub state if available
  - propose ordered remediation
  - do not apply remote changes without explicit approval
- Approval gates:
  - required for `gh api` repo settings updates
  - required for ruleset create/update
  - required for workflows that change branch governance behavior
- Forbidden without explicit user request:
  - destructive git commands
  - bypassing hooks
  - secret injection
  - assuming GitHub-only behavior in the neutral core

**Output Contract**
- Primary outputs:
  - `delta_report.md`
  - `execution_log.json`
  - `check_results.md`
- Report sections:
  - repo maturity classification
  - compliant baseline items
  - missing/misaligned items
  - local changes planned/applied
  - remote changes planned/applied
  - validation results
  - residual risks / follow-ups

**Recommended Skill Package**
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/SKILL.md`
  - short triggerable entrypoint with workflows and guardrails
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/baseline-rubric.md`
  - distilled “good looks like” rubric
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/maturity-profiles.md`
  - docs-first vs implementation-phase rules
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/github-governance.md`
  - `gh api` inspection/apply/verify patterns, rulesets, approval rules
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/adoption-sources.md`
  - how `base-repo-template` and `forge-program-tracker` inform the baseline
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/delta-report-template.md`
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/check-results-template.md`
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/execution-log-template.json.md`
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/preflight-checklist.md`
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/verification-checklist.md`

**What `SKILL.md` Should Emphasize**
- start by classifying maturity
- use the baseline rubric, not blind file copying
- keep repo instructions concise and repo-specific
- prefer deterministic commands and explicit outputs
- separate neutral core from GitHub adapter details
- re-audit after changes; don’t claim success without verification

**Baseline Criteria The Skill Should Enforce**
- repo intent/context docs exist and reflect reality
- governance docs/templates are present and repo-specific
- `pre-commit` baseline exists and is runnable
- CI preserves required checks even for docs-first repos
- release automation is scaffolded honestly
- remote rules/settings are auditable as code
- `AGENTS.md` enables safe AI operation
- environment/integration surfaces are documented where relevant

**Implementation Plan**
1. distill `base-repo-template` + `forge-program-tracker` into neutral baseline references
2. write a tight `SKILL.md` optimized for triggerability
3. add deterministic templates/checklists
4. isolate GitHub-specific remote operations into references
5. validate the package with local skill scripts
6. update docs inventory if the skill is added to this repo

**Tradeoff I’d choose**
- Prefer a concise `SKILL.md` plus strong references over a giant self-contained skill file.
- That matches the best parts of your handoff while avoiding the “too much in one place” problem.

If you want, next I can turn this into a file-by-file draft outline with the exact sections I’d put into each file before any implementation starts.

diffs.after:
# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap Planning Handoff

This folder captures the planning state needed to build the new `ai-native-repo-bootstrap` skill.

## Objective

Create a reusable skill at `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap` that bootstraps or upgrades a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern developer experience guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.

## Current Decisions

- Skill name: `ai-native-repo-bootstrap`
- Package depth: full package, not MVP-only
- Core positioning: get a target repo ready for AI-native development
- Primary workflows: `new_repo` and `upgrade_repo`
- Vendor posture: vendor-neutral core with GitHub adapter/reference guidance
- Remote operations policy: audit first, require explicit approval before any `gh api` apply phase

## Source Materials

The skill should be derived from these sources, in this priority order:

1. `temp/template-target-state-handoff.md`
2. `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
3. `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
4. `AGENTS.md`
5. `.agents/skills/skill-builder-auditor/*`

## What Good Looks Like

The target skill should optimize for these outcomes:

- Repo intent is legible to both humans and AI agents.
- Governance and contribution standards are explicit early.
- Local feedback loops exist through `pre-commit` and markdown hygiene.
- CI preserves required quality checks even for docs-first repositories.
- Release automation is scaffolded honestly for current repo maturity.
- Remote GitHub policy is treated as code and can be audited against live state.
- AI operating instructions are concise, repo-specific, and actionable.
- Maturity-based exceptions are intentional rather than accidental.

## Key Insight From Source Repos

`base-repo-template` defines the generic governance baseline.

`forge-program-tracker` is the stronger target-state example because it shows how to adapt that baseline for a real docs-first, AI-assisted repository with policy drift detection, remote ruleset sync, and repo-specific agent instructions.

The skill should therefore model the delta between those two repositories rather than blindly copying the base template.

## Planned Deliverable Shape

The skill package should include:

- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/SKILL.md`
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/*.md`
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/*.md`
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/*.md`
- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/agents/openai.yaml`

See `docs/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/skill-spec.md` for the contract and `docs/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/file-plan.md` for the file-by-file plan.

## Recommended Build Order

1. Distill the neutral baseline from the source materials.
2. Write the concise `SKILL.md` entrypoint.
3. Add reference documents for maturity profiles and GitHub governance.
4. Add deterministic templates and checklists.
5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml`.
6. Validate the skill and docs inventory.

## Validation Commands

Run these from the repository root as work progresses:

```bash
uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap

# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap Skill Specification

## Purpose

Bootstrap or upgrade a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern developer experience guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.

## Positioning

This skill is marketed as a way to get a target repository ready for AI-native development.

It should not present itself as a generic template copier. It should present itself as a guided repo adoption and hardening workflow.

## Trigger Conditions

Use this skill when the user asks to:

- bootstrap a repo for AI-native development
- prepare a repo so AI agents can work well
- bring a repo up to a modern devex baseline
- upgrade a repo to stronger guardrails and feedback loops
- align a repo with the repository baseline or target-state template

Do not trigger for isolated app feature work or one-off lint or CI fixes with no repo-bootstrap intent.

## Workflows

### `new_repo`

Use when the repository is empty, lightly scaffolded, or still near-template state.

Outcome:

- required baseline files exist
- placeholders are replaced or intentionally documented
- local quality loops run
- CI and release scaffolding are in place
- remote GitHub parity can be audited and proposed

### `upgrade_repo`

Use when the repository already has established structure and needs baseline alignment.

Outcome:

- current local and remote state are audited
- deltas are classified by severity
- missing baseline items are added or updated
- maturity-based exceptions are preserved where intentional
- remote GitHub parity changes are planned or applied only with approval

## Scope Boundaries

### In Scope

- repository baseline audit
- repo-specific documentation and governance scaffolding
- local quality gates
- CI and release scaffolding
- GitHub ruleset and settings audit planning
- maturity-aware adaptation for docs-first repositories
- final delta and verification reporting

### Out of Scope

- product feature implementation
- secret provisioning
- organization-wide GitHub administration unrelated to the target repository
- destructive git cleanup
- silent remote GitHub mutation

## Operating Principles

- Start by classifying repository maturity before making recommendations.
- Use the baseline rubric instead of blindly copying files.
- Prefer deterministic outputs, concrete commands, and explicit report sections.
- Keep the neutral core separate from GitHub-specific operational guidance.
- Re-audit after changes and report residual gaps honestly.

## Maturity Profiles

The skill should explicitly support at least these repository states:

1. Docs-first planning repository
2. Early implementation repository
3. Active delivery repository

Each profile may justify different recommendations for tests, release stamping, language-specific hooks, and environment documentation.

## Approval Boundaries

The skill may inspect local files and propose changes without a special approval step.

The skill must require explicit user approval before:

- applying GitHub repository settings with `gh api`
- creating or updating GitHub rulesets
- changing remote governance or required checks
- taking any destructive action

## Output Contract

Each run should produce a clear report with these sections:

- repository maturity classification
- compliant baseline items
- missing or misaligned baseline items
- local changes planned or applied
- remote changes planned or applied
- validation results
- residual risks and follow-ups

## Baseline Rubric Categories

The skill should evaluate at minimum:

1. Repo intent and documentation clarity
2. Governance and contribution standards
3. Local feedback loops
4. CI quality enforcement
5. Release scaffolding
6. Remote GitHub policy as code
7. AI operability instructions
8. Environment and integration documentation
9. Maturity-based exceptions

## Required Skill Outputs

The finished skill package should support generating or reasoning about these artifact classes:

- required repo files and their target states
- repo delta reports
- execution logs
- preflight checklists
- verification checklists
- GitHub remote audit and apply sequences

## Canonical Examples

Use these repositories as canonical examples while authoring:

- Generic baseline: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
- AI-native target-state example: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`

The strongest guidance should come from the differences between them.

+# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap Planning Handoff
+
+This folder captures the planning state needed to build the new `ai-native-repo-bootstrap` skill.
+
+## Objective
+
+Create a reusable skill at `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap` that bootstraps or upgrades a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern developer experience guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.
+
+## Current Decisions
+
+- Skill name: `ai-native-repo-bootstrap`
+- Package depth: full package, not MVP-only
+- Core positioning: get a target repo ready for AI-native development
+- Primary workflows: `new_repo` and `upgrade_repo`
+- Vendor posture: vendor-neutral core with GitHub adapter/reference guidance
+- Remote operations policy: audit first, require explicit approval before any `gh api` apply phase
+
+## Source Materials
+
+The skill should be derived from these sources, in this priority order:
+
+1. `temp/template-target-state-handoff.md`
+2. `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
+3. `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
+4. `AGENTS.md`
+5. `.agents/skills/skill-builder-auditor/*`
+
+## What Good Looks Like
+
+The target skill should optimize for these outcomes:
+
+- Repo intent is legible to both humans and AI agents.
+- Governance and contribution standards are explicit early.
+- Local feedback loops exist through `pre-commit` and markdown hygiene.
+- CI preserves required quality checks even for docs-first repositories.
+- Release automation is scaffolded honestly for current repo maturity.
+- Remote GitHub policy is treated as code and can be audited against live state.
+- AI operating instructions are concise, repo-specific, and actionable.
+- Maturity-based exceptions are intentional rather than accidental.
+
+## Key Insight From Source Repos
+
+`base-repo-template` defines the generic governance baseline.
+
+`forge-program-tracker` is the stronger target-state example because it shows how to adapt that baseline for a real docs-first, AI-assisted repository with policy drift detection, remote ruleset sync, and repo-specific agent instructions.
+
+The skill should therefore model the delta between those two repositories rather than blindly copying the base template.
+
+## Planned Deliverable Shape
+
+The skill package should include:
+
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/SKILL.md`
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/*.md`
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/*.md`
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/*.md`
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/agents/openai.yaml`
+
+See `temp/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/skill-spec.md` for the contract and `temp/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/file-plan.md` for the file-by-file plan.
+
+## Recommended Build Order
+
+1. Distill the neutral baseline from the source materials.
+2. Write the concise `SKILL.md` entrypoint.
+3. Add reference documents for maturity profiles and GitHub governance.
+4. Add deterministic templates and checklists.
+5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml`.
+6. Validate the skill and docs inventory.
+
+## Validation Commands
+
+Run these from the repository root as work progresses:
+
+```bash
+uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+python scripts/check_docs_drift.py
+```
+
+## Resume Checklist
+
+When resuming on another machine:
+
+1. Open `temp/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/README.md`.
+2. Review `temp/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/skill-spec.md`.
+3. Review `temp/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/file-plan.md`.
+4. Start authoring the skill package under `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
+5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml` after `SKILL.md` is stable.
+6. Run validation commands before committing.

+# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap Skill Specification
+
+## Purpose
+
+Bootstrap or upgrade a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern developer experience guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.
+
+## Positioning
+
+This skill is marketed as a way to get a target repository ready for AI-native development.
+
+It should not present itself as a generic template copier. It should present itself as a guided repo adoption and hardening workflow.
+
+## Trigger Conditions
+
+Use this skill when the user asks to:
+
+- bootstrap a repo for AI-native development
+- prepare a repo so AI agents can work well
+- bring a repo up to a modern devex baseline
+- upgrade a repo to stronger guardrails and feedback loops
+- align a repo with the repository baseline or target-state template
+
+Do not trigger for isolated app feature work or one-off lint or CI fixes with no repo-bootstrap intent.
+
+## Workflows
+
+### `new_repo`
+
+Use when the repository is empty, lightly scaffolded, or still near-template state.
+
+Outcome:
+
+- required baseline files exist
+- placeholders are replaced or intentionally documented
+- local quality loops run
+- CI and release scaffolding are in place
+- remote GitHub parity can be audited and proposed
+
+### `upgrade_repo`
+
+Use when the repository already has established structure and needs baseline alignment.
+
+Outcome:
+
+- current local and remote state are audited
+- deltas are classified by severity
+- missing baseline items are added or updated
+- maturity-based exceptions are preserved where intentional
+- remote GitHub parity changes are planned or applied only with approval
+
+## Scope Boundaries
+
+### In Scope
+
+- repository baseline audit
+- repo-specific documentation and governance scaffolding
+- local quality gates
+- CI and release scaffolding
+- GitHub ruleset and settings audit planning
+- maturity-aware adaptation for docs-first repositories
+- final delta and verification reporting
+
+### Out of Scope
+
+- product feature implementation
+- secret provisioning
+- organization-wide GitHub administration unrelated to the target repository
+- destructive git cleanup
+- silent remote GitHub mutation
+
+## Operating Principles
+
+- Start by classifying repository maturity before making recommendations.
+- Use the baseline rubric instead of blindly copying files.
+- Prefer deterministic outputs, concrete commands, and explicit report sections.
+- Keep the neutral core separate from GitHub-specific operational guidance.
+- Re-audit after changes and report residual gaps honestly.
+
+## Maturity Profiles
+
+The skill should explicitly support at least these repository states:
+
+1. Docs-first planning repository
+2. Early implementation repository
+3. Active delivery repository
+
+Each profile may justify different recommendations for tests, release stamping, language-specific hooks, and environment documentation.
+
+## Approval Boundaries
+
+The skill may inspect local files and propose changes without a special approval step.
+
+The skill must require explicit user approval before:
+
+- applying GitHub repository settings with `gh api`
+- creating or updating GitHub rulesets
+- changing remote governance or required checks
+- taking any destructive action
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Each run should produce a clear report with these sections:
+
+- repository maturity classification
+- compliant baseline items
+- missing or misaligned baseline items
+- local changes planned or applied
+- remote changes planned or applied
+- validation results
+- residual risks and follow-ups
+
+## Baseline Rubric Categories
+
+The skill should evaluate at minimum:
+
+1. Repo intent and documentation clarity
+2. Governance and contribution standards
+3. Local feedback loops
+4. CI quality enforcement
+5. Release scaffolding
+6. Remote GitHub policy as code
+7. AI operability instructions
+8. Environment and integration documentation
+9. Maturity-based exceptions
+
+## Required Skill Outputs
+
+The finished skill package should support generating or reasoning about these artifact classes:
+
+- required repo files and their target states
+- repo delta reports
+- execution logs
+- preflight checklists
+- verification checklists
+- GitHub remote audit and apply sequences
+
+## Canonical Examples
+
+Use these repositories as canonical examples while authoring:
+
+- Generic baseline: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
+- AI-native target-state example: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
+
+The strongest guidance should come from the differences between them.

+

+

+

+

+

+- workflow summary
+- approval boundaries
+- concise execution contract
+

+- when not to use this skill

+- workflow: `upgrade_repo`
+- output expectations
+- approval and safety rules
+- validation expectations
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/baseline-rubric.md`
+

+

+
+Key sections:
+
+- rubric categories
+- required artifacts
+- optional artifacts
+- minimum acceptable state
+- strong target state
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/maturity-profiles.md`
+

+

+

+
+- profile definitions
+- allowed exceptions
+- anti-patterns
+- escalation points
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/github-governance.md`
+

+

+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/adoption-sources.md`
+

+- target repo takeaways
+- translation rules into this skill
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/delta-report-template.md`
+

+

+

+
+- repo summary
+- maturity classification
+- compliant items
+- gaps by severity
+- local remediation plan
+- remote remediation plan
+- validation results
+- follow-ups
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/check-results-template.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- standardize validation reporting
+
+Suggested sections:
+
+- commands run
+- pass or fail results
+- known skips
+- unresolved blockers
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/execution-log-template.json.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- define the expected shape of a structured execution log without forcing a real JSON artifact in the skill package itself
+
+Suggested fields:
+
+- timestamp
+- repository path
+- workflow mode
+- actions taken
+- approvals received
+- local files changed
+- remote operations proposed or applied
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/preflight-checklist.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- ensure safe startup conditions before making changes
+
+Checklist topics:
+
+- clean working tree expectations
+- auth and tool availability
+- repository maturity identification
+- repo path confirmation
+- remote approval boundary confirmation
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/verification-checklist.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- ensure work is verified after changes
+
+Checklist topics:
+
+- local checks pass
+- docs are internally consistent
+- CI and release scaffolding are coherent
+- remote parity audit completed
+- residual risks documented
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/agents/openai.yaml`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- generated agent metadata for this repository
+
+Generation step:
+
+```bash
+uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+```
+
+## Implementation Sequence
+
+1. Create the folder scaffold under `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
+2. Write `SKILL.md` with a concise, triggerable contract.
+3. Add the reference documents.
+4. Add templates and checklists.
+5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml`.
+6. Validate the new skill.
+7. Update docs inventory if needed.
+
+## Validation Sequence
+
+```bash
+uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+python scripts/check_docs_drift.py
+```
+
+## Open Questions To Resolve During Build
+
+- Whether the first version should include an explicit repository settings adapter beyond rulesets.
+- Whether agent runtime config files such as `.claude/`, `.cursor/`, and `.opencode/` should be treated as recommended or optional-plus.
+- Whether the skill should emit only markdown reports or also prescribe a JSON report contract.
+- Whether docs inventory updates belong in the same first implementation commit or a follow-up commit.

# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap Skill Specification

## Purpose

Bootstrap or upgrade a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern developer experience guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.

## Positioning

This skill is marketed as a way to get a target repository ready for AI-native development.

It should not present itself as a generic template copier. It should present itself as a guided repo adoption and hardening workflow.

## Trigger Conditions

Use this skill when the user asks to:

- bootstrap a repo for AI-native development
- prepare a repo so AI agents can work well
- bring a repo up to a modern devex baseline
- upgrade a repo to stronger guardrails and feedback loops
- align a repo with the repository baseline or target-state template

Do not trigger for isolated app feature work or one-off lint or CI fixes with no repo-bootstrap intent.

## Workflows

### `new_repo`

Use when the repository is empty, lightly scaffolded, or still near-template state.

Outcome:

- required baseline files exist
- placeholders are replaced or intentionally documented
- local quality loops run
- CI and release scaffolding are in place
- remote GitHub parity can be audited and proposed

### `upgrade_repo`

Use when the repository already has established structure and needs baseline alignment.

Outcome:

- current local and remote state are audited
- deltas are classified by severity
- missing baseline items are added or updated
- maturity-based exceptions are preserved where intentional
- remote GitHub parity changes are planned or applied only with approval

## Scope Boundaries

### In Scope

- repository baseline audit
- repo-specific documentation and governance scaffolding
- local quality gates
- CI and release scaffolding
- GitHub ruleset and settings audit planning
- maturity-aware adaptation for docs-first repositories
- final delta and verification reporting

### Out of Scope

- product feature implementation
- secret provisioning
- organization-wide GitHub administration unrelated to the target repository
- destructive git cleanup
- silent remote GitHub mutation

## Operating Principles

- Start by classifying repository maturity before making recommendations.
- Use the baseline rubric instead of blindly copying files.
- Prefer deterministic outputs, concrete commands, and explicit report sections.
- Keep the neutral core separate from GitHub-specific operational guidance.
- Re-audit after changes and report residual gaps honestly.

## Maturity Profiles

The skill should explicitly support at least these repository states:

1. Docs-first planning repository
2. Early implementation repository
3. Active delivery repository

Each profile may justify different recommendations for tests, release stamping, language-specific hooks, and environment documentation.

## Approval Boundaries

The skill may inspect local files and propose changes without a special approval step.

The skill must require explicit user approval before:

- applying GitHub repository settings with `gh api`
- creating or updating GitHub rulesets
- changing remote governance or required checks
- taking any destructive action

## Output Contract

Each run should produce a clear report with these sections:

- repository maturity classification
- compliant baseline items
- missing or misaligned baseline items
- local changes planned or applied
- remote changes planned or applied
- validation results
- residual risks and follow-ups

## Baseline Rubric Categories

The skill should evaluate at minimum:

1. Repo intent and documentation clarity
2. Governance and contribution standards
3. Local feedback loops
4. CI quality enforcement
5. Release scaffolding
6. Remote GitHub policy as code
7. AI operability instructions
8. Environment and integration documentation
9. Maturity-based exceptions

## Required Skill Outputs

The finished skill package should support generating or reasoning about these artifact classes:

- required repo files and their target states
- repo delta reports
- execution logs
- preflight checklists
- verification checklists
- GitHub remote audit and apply sequences

## Canonical Examples

Use these repositories as canonical examples while authoring:

- Generic baseline: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
- AI-native target-state example: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`

The strongest guidance should come from the differences between them.

+uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+python scripts/check_docs_drift.py

+
+## Open Questions To Resolve During Build

15: - Vendor posture: vendor-neutral core with GitHub adapter/reference guidance
16: - Remote operations policy: audit first, require explicit approval before any `gh api` apply phase
17: 
18: ## Source Materials
19: 
20: The skill should be derived from these sources, in this priority order:
21: 
22: 1. `temp/template-target-state-handoff.md`
23: 2. `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
24: 3. `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
25: 4. `AGENTS.md`
26: 5. `.agents/skills/skill-builder-auditor/*`
27: 
28: ## What Good Looks Like
29: 
30: The target skill should optimize for these outcomes:
31: 
32: - Repo intent is legible to both humans and AI agents.
33: - Governance and contribution standards are explicit early.
34: - Local feedback loops exist through `pre-commit` and markdown hygiene.
35: - CI preserves required quality checks even for docs-first repositories.
36: - Release automation is scaffolded honestly for current repo maturity.
37: - Remote GitHub policy is treated as code and can be audited against live state.
38: - AI operating instructions are concise, repo-specific, and actionable.
39: - Maturity-based exceptions are intentional rather than accidental.
40: 
41: ## Key Insight From Source Repos
42: 
43: `base-repo-template` defines the generic governance baseline.
44: 
45: `forge-program-tracker` is the stronger target-state example because it shows how to adapt that baseline for a real docs-first, AI-assisted repository with policy drift detection, remote ruleset sync, and repo-specific agent instructions.
46: 
47: The skill should therefore model the delta between those two repositories rather than blindly copying the base template.
48:

60:

62: 
63: 1. Distill the neutral baseline from the source materials.
64: 2. Write the concise `SKILL.md` entrypoint.
65: 3. Add reference documents for maturity profiles and GitHub governance.
66: 4. Add deterministic templates and checklists.
67: 5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml`.
68: 6. Validate the skill and docs inventory.
69: 
70: ## Validation Commands
71: 
72: Run these from the repository root as work progresses:
73: 
74: ```bash
75: uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
76: uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
77: python scripts/check_docs_drift.py
78: ```
79: 
80: ## Resume Checklist
81: 
82: When resuming on another machine:
83: 
84: 1. Open `temp/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/README.md`.
85: 2. Review `temp/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/skill-spec.md`.
86: 3. Review `temp/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/file-plan.md`.
87: 4. Start authoring the skill package under `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
88: 5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml` after `SKILL.md` is stable.
89: 6. Run validation commands before committing.

(End of file - total 89 lines)
</content>

+# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap Planning Handoff
+
+This folder captures the planning state needed to build the new `ai-native-repo-bootstrap` skill.
+
+## Objective
+
+Create a reusable skill at `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap` that bootstraps or upgrades a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern developer experience guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.
+
+## Current Decisions
+
+- Skill name: `ai-native-repo-bootstrap`
+- Package depth: full package, not MVP-only
+- Core positioning: get a target repo ready for AI-native development
+- Primary workflows: `new_repo` and `upgrade_repo`
+- Vendor posture: vendor-neutral core with GitHub adapter/reference guidance
+- Remote operations policy: audit first, require explicit approval before any `gh api` apply phase
+
+## Source Materials
+
+The skill should be derived from these sources, in this priority order:
+
+1. `temp/template-target-state-handoff.md`
+2. `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
+3. `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
+4. `AGENTS.md`
+5. `.agents/skills/skill-builder-auditor/*`
+
+## What Good Looks Like
+
+The target skill should optimize for these outcomes:
+
+- Repo intent is legible to both humans and AI agents.
+- Governance and contribution standards are explicit early.
+- Local feedback loops exist through `pre-commit` and markdown hygiene.
+- CI preserves required quality checks even for docs-first repositories.
+- Release automation is scaffolded honestly for current repo maturity.
+- Remote GitHub policy is treated as code and can be audited against live state.
+- AI operating instructions are concise, repo-specific, and actionable.
+- Maturity-based exceptions are intentional rather than accidental.
+
+## Key Insight From Source Repos
+
+`base-repo-template` defines the generic governance baseline.
+
+`forge-program-tracker` is the stronger target-state example because it shows how to adapt that baseline for a real docs-first, AI-assisted repository with policy drift detection, remote ruleset sync, and repo-specific agent instructions.
+
+The skill should therefore model the delta between those two repositories rather than blindly copying the base template.
+
+## Planned Deliverable Shape
+
+The skill package should include:
+
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/SKILL.md`
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/*.md`
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/*.md`
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/*.md`
+- `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/agents/openai.yaml`
+
+See `docs/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/skill-spec.md` for the contract and `docs/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/file-plan.md` for the file-by-file plan.
+
+## Recommended Build Order
+
+1. Distill the neutral baseline from the source materials.
+2. Write the concise `SKILL.md` entrypoint.
+3. Add reference documents for maturity profiles and GitHub governance.
+4. Add deterministic templates and checklists.
+5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml`.
+6. Validate the skill and docs inventory.
+
+## Validation Commands
+
+Run these from the repository root as work progresses:
+
+```bash
+uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+python scripts/check_docs_drift.py
+```
+
+## Resume Checklist
+
+When resuming on another machine:
+
+1. Open `docs/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/README.md`.
+2. Review `docs/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/skill-spec.md`.
+3. Review `docs/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/file-plan.md`.
+4. Start authoring the skill package under `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
+5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml` after `SKILL.md` is stable.
+6. Run validation commands before committing.
*** Add File: /home/damien/Liatrio/repos/ai-prompts/docs/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/skill-spec.md
+# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap Skill Specification
+
+## Purpose
+
+Bootstrap or upgrade a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern developer experience guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.
+
+## Positioning
+
+This skill is marketed as a way to get a target repository ready for AI-native development.
+
+It should not present itself as a generic template copier. It should present itself as a guided repo adoption and hardening workflow.
+
+## Trigger Conditions
+
+Use this skill when the user asks to:
+
+- bootstrap a repo for AI-native development
+- prepare a repo so AI agents can work well
+- bring a repo up to a modern devex baseline
+- upgrade a repo to stronger guardrails and feedback loops
+- align a repo with the repository baseline or target-state template
+
+Do not trigger for isolated app feature work or one-off lint or CI fixes with no repo-bootstrap intent.
+
+## Workflows
+
+### `new_repo`
+
+Use when the repository is empty, lightly scaffolded, or still near-template state.
+
+Outcome:
+
+- required baseline files exist
+- placeholders are replaced or intentionally documented
+- local quality loops run
+- CI and release scaffolding are in place
+- remote GitHub parity can be audited and proposed
+
+### `upgrade_repo`
+
+Use when the repository already has established structure and needs baseline alignment.
+
+Outcome:
+
+- current local and remote state are audited
+- deltas are classified by severity
+- missing baseline items are added or updated
+- maturity-based exceptions are preserved where intentional
+- remote GitHub parity changes are planned or applied only with approval
+
+## Scope Boundaries
+
+### In Scope
+
+- repository baseline audit
+- repo-specific documentation and governance scaffolding
+- local quality gates
+- CI and release scaffolding
+- GitHub ruleset and settings audit planning
+- maturity-aware adaptation for docs-first repositories
+- final delta and verification reporting
+
+### Out of Scope
+
+- product feature implementation
+- secret provisioning
+- organization-wide GitHub administration unrelated to the target repository
+- destructive git cleanup
+- silent remote GitHub mutation
+
+## Operating Principles
+
+- Start by classifying repository maturity before making recommendations.
+- Use the baseline rubric instead of blindly copying files.
+- Prefer deterministic outputs, concrete commands, and explicit report sections.
+- Keep the neutral core separate from GitHub-specific operational guidance.
+- Re-audit after changes and report residual gaps honestly.
+
+## Maturity Profiles
+
+The skill should explicitly support at least these repository states:
+
+1. Docs-first planning repository
+2. Early implementation repository
+3. Active delivery repository
+
+Each profile may justify different recommendations for tests, release stamping, language-specific hooks, and environment documentation.
+
+## Approval Boundaries
+
+The skill may inspect local files and propose changes without a special approval step.
+
+The skill must require explicit user approval before:
+
+- applying GitHub repository settings with `gh api`
+- creating or updating GitHub rulesets
+- changing remote governance or required checks
+- taking any destructive action
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Each run should produce a clear report with these sections:
+
+- repository maturity classification
+- compliant baseline items
+- missing or misaligned baseline items
+- local changes planned or applied
+- remote changes planned or applied
+- validation results
+- residual risks and follow-ups
+
+## Baseline Rubric Categories
+
+The skill should evaluate at minimum:
+
+1. Repo intent and documentation clarity
+2. Governance and contribution standards
+3. Local feedback loops
+4. CI quality enforcement
+5. Release scaffolding
+6. Remote GitHub policy as code
+7. AI operability instructions
+8. Environment and integration documentation
+9. Maturity-based exceptions
+
+## Required Skill Outputs
+
+The finished skill package should support generating or reasoning about these artifact classes:
+
+- required repo files and their target states
+- repo delta reports
+- execution logs
+- preflight checklists
+- verification checklists
+- GitHub remote audit and apply sequences
+
+## Canonical Examples
+
+Use these repositories as canonical examples while authoring:
+
+- Generic baseline: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
+- AI-native target-state example: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
+
+The strongest guidance should come from the differences between them.

+# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap Skill Specification
+
+## Purpose
+
+Bootstrap or upgrade a repository so humans and AI agents can work effectively with clear context, modern developer experience guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance.
+
+## Positioning
+
+This skill is marketed as a way to get a target repository ready for AI-native development.
+
+It should not present itself as a generic template copier. It should present itself as a guided repo adoption and hardening workflow.
+
+## Trigger Conditions
+
+Use this skill when the user asks to:
+
+- bootstrap a repo for AI-native development
+- prepare a repo so AI agents can work well
+- bring a repo up to a modern devex baseline
+- upgrade a repo to stronger guardrails and feedback loops
+- align a repo with the repository baseline or target-state template
+
+Do not trigger for isolated app feature work or one-off lint or CI fixes with no repo-bootstrap intent.
+
+## Workflows
+
+### `new_repo`
+
+Use when the repository is empty, lightly scaffolded, or still near-template state.
+
+Outcome:
+
+- required baseline files exist
+- placeholders are replaced or intentionally documented
+- local quality loops run
+- CI and release scaffolding are in place
+- remote GitHub parity can be audited and proposed
+
+### `upgrade_repo`
+
+Use when the repository already has established structure and needs baseline alignment.
+
+Outcome:
+
+- current local and remote state are audited
+- deltas are classified by severity
+- missing baseline items are added or updated
+- maturity-based exceptions are preserved where intentional
+- remote GitHub parity changes are planned or applied only with approval
+
+## Scope Boundaries
+
+### In Scope
+
+- repository baseline audit
+- repo-specific documentation and governance scaffolding
+- local quality gates
+- CI and release scaffolding
+- GitHub ruleset and settings audit planning
+- maturity-aware adaptation for docs-first repositories
+- final delta and verification reporting
+
+### Out of Scope
+
+- product feature implementation
+- secret provisioning
+- organization-wide GitHub administration unrelated to the target repository
+- destructive git cleanup
+- silent remote GitHub mutation
+
+## Operating Principles
+
+- Start by classifying repository maturity before making recommendations.
+- Use the baseline rubric instead of blindly copying files.
+- Prefer deterministic outputs, concrete commands, and explicit report sections.
+- Keep the neutral core separate from GitHub-specific operational guidance.
+- Re-audit after changes and report residual gaps honestly.
+
+## Maturity Profiles
+
+The skill should explicitly support at least these repository states:
+
+1. Docs-first planning repository
+2. Early implementation repository
+3. Active delivery repository
+
+Each profile may justify different recommendations for tests, release stamping, language-specific hooks, and environment documentation.
+
+## Approval Boundaries
+
+The skill may inspect local files and propose changes without a special approval step.
+
+The skill must require explicit user approval before:
+
+- applying GitHub repository settings with `gh api`
+- creating or updating GitHub rulesets
+- changing remote governance or required checks
+- taking any destructive action
+
+## Output Contract
+
+Each run should produce a clear report with these sections:
+
+- repository maturity classification
+- compliant baseline items
+- missing or misaligned baseline items
+- local changes planned or applied
+- remote changes planned or applied
+- validation results
+- residual risks and follow-ups
+
+## Baseline Rubric Categories
+
+The skill should evaluate at minimum:
+
+1. Repo intent and documentation clarity
+2. Governance and contribution standards
+3. Local feedback loops
+4. CI quality enforcement
+5. Release scaffolding
+6. Remote GitHub policy as code
+7. AI operability instructions
+8. Environment and integration documentation
+9. Maturity-based exceptions
+
+## Required Skill Outputs
+
+The finished skill package should support generating or reasoning about these artifact classes:
+
+- required repo files and their target states
+- repo delta reports
+- execution logs
+- preflight checklists
+- verification checklists
+- GitHub remote audit and apply sequences
+
+## Canonical Examples
+
+Use these repositories as canonical examples while authoring:
+
+- Generic baseline: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
+- AI-native target-state example: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
+
+The strongest guidance should come from the differences between them.

+# AI-Native Repo Bootstrap File Plan
+
+## Skill Package Target
+
+Create the package at `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
+
+## Planned Files
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/SKILL.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- primary skill entrypoint
+- trigger language
+- workflow summary
+- approval boundaries
+- concise execution contract
+
+Key sections:
+
+- frontmatter with name and description
+- when to use this skill
+- when not to use this skill
+- workflow: `new_repo`
+- workflow: `upgrade_repo`
+- output expectations
+- approval and safety rules
+- validation expectations
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/baseline-rubric.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- capture the canonical definition of a good AI-native repository baseline
+
+Key sections:
+
+- rubric categories
+- required artifacts
+- optional artifacts
+- minimum acceptable state
+- strong target state
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/maturity-profiles.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- explain how recommendations change for docs-first, early implementation, and active delivery repositories
+
+Key sections:
+
+- profile definitions
+- allowed exceptions
+- anti-patterns
+- escalation points
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/github-governance.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- isolate GitHub-specific remote operations from the neutral core
+
+Key sections:
+
+- repo settings audit commands
+- ruleset audit commands
+- ruleset apply commands
+- drift detection patterns
+- approval requirements
+- verification steps
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/references/adoption-sources.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- explain how source repositories informed the final baseline so future maintainers can understand provenance
+
+Key sections:
+
+- source inventory
+- baseline repo takeaways
+- target repo takeaways
+- translation rules into this skill
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/delta-report-template.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- provide a deterministic report structure for audits and upgrade runs
+
+Suggested sections:
+
+- repo summary
+- maturity classification
+- compliant items
+- gaps by severity
+- local remediation plan
+- remote remediation plan
+- validation results
+- follow-ups
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/check-results-template.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- standardize validation reporting
+
+Suggested sections:
+
+- commands run
+- pass or fail results
+- known skips
+- unresolved blockers
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/templates/execution-log-template.json.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- define the expected shape of a structured execution log without forcing a real JSON artifact in the skill package itself
+
+Suggested fields:
+
+- timestamp
+- repository path
+- workflow mode
+- actions taken
+- approvals received
+- local files changed
+- remote operations proposed or applied
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/preflight-checklist.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- ensure safe startup conditions before making changes
+
+Checklist topics:
+
+- clean working tree expectations
+- auth and tool availability
+- repository maturity identification
+- repo path confirmation
+- remote approval boundary confirmation
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/checklists/verification-checklist.md`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- ensure work is verified after changes
+
+Checklist topics:
+
+- local checks pass
+- docs are internally consistent
+- CI and release scaffolding are coherent
+- remote parity audit completed
+- residual risks documented
+
+### `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap/agents/openai.yaml`
+
+Purpose:
+
+- generated agent metadata for this repository
+
+Generation step:
+
+```bash
+uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+```
+
+## Implementation Sequence
+
+1. Create the folder scaffold under `skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap`.
+2. Write `SKILL.md` with a concise, triggerable contract.
+3. Add the reference documents.
+4. Add templates and checklists.
+5. Generate `agents/openai.yaml`.
+6. Validate the new skill.
+7. Update docs inventory if needed.
+
+## Validation Sequence
+
+```bash
+uv run scripts/generate_openai_yaml.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+uv run scripts/quick_validate.py skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap
+python scripts/check_docs_drift.py
+```
+
+## Open Questions To Resolve During Build
+
+- Whether the first version should include an explicit repository settings adapter beyond rulesets.
+- Whether agent runtime config files such as `.claude/`, `.cursor/`, and `.opencode/` should be treated as recommended or optional-plus.
+- Whether the skill should emit only markdown reports or also prescribe a JSON report contract.
+- Whether docs inventory updates belong in the same first implementation commit or a follow-up commit.
